# TITLE XXVII.

## CONVEYANCES.

---

### 1862.
*February 25.*

**1** AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to cause writings to be made in the English language."

1 Stanton's R. S. 286.
How deeds and powers of attorney in foreign languages may be admitted to record.

**SEC. I.** That the act, approved March 10, 1856, entitled "an act to cause writings to be made in the English language," be, and is hereby, so amended as that deeds and powers of attorney, written in any other language than the English, shall be admitted to record as deeds and powers of attorney are now admitted to record: *Provided*, That the person offering the same for record shall produce a correct English translation thereof; and the translation shall be filed with the deed or power of attorney, and, together with the said deed or power of attorney, be recorded in the deed book or power of attorney book.

**SEC. II.** This act shall apply to deeds and powers of attorney heretofore executed, and shall take effect from its passage.

Marriage license

**SEC. I.** That if the female, with whom a marriage is intended to be had, is a resident of any county in which there are no officers to grant the license, it shall be lawful for the proper officer of any other county to issue such license according to the requirements of the law now in force, except in regard to residence; and the certificate of the marriage shall be returned to, and recorded in the county from which the license is issued: *Provided*, That the fact that there are no officers authorized to grant license in the county of the residence, shall appear by the affidavit of the parent or guardian of the former, if she is an infant, and by her own and the affidavit of her intended husband, if she is an adult, which affidavits shall be filed by the clerk: *And provided further*, That the application must be made in the nearest county where there are officers authorized to grant the license.

**SEC. II.** This act shall be in force from its passage

### I. *Of wife's choses in action, personalty and slaves.*

1. *What law governs right of husband to wife's movables.*—The right of the husband to the movables, including slaves, owned by the wife at the time of the marriage, is determined by the law of the place where they were married, although they may afterward remove with the property to another domicile. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352.

as they (the council) should designate: *Held*—that this did not authorize a tax on *choses in action*. *City of Covington v. Powell*, 2 Met. 226.

5. *To create lien for taxes*—*what requisite*.—To enable a municipal corporation to enforce a lien for taxes on the property of a citizen, there must be a strict compliance with the prerequisites of the statute conferring the lien. *City of Louisville v. Bank of Kentucky*, 3 Met. 148.

6. *Billiards, how licensed*.—By section three of the act of February 9, 1858, the municipal authorities of cities and towns have the exclusive right to license billiard tables within their limits, to prescribe the rates to be paid, and the number of tables to be set up. And if the grantee be licensed to keep more than one table, still he is only bound under the proviso of the above section to pay to the clerk of the county court $100. *Metz v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 14.

# TITLE CIX.

## ROADS AND PASSWAYS.

### 1860.
*March 2.*

*[Road running partly in two counties. Jurisdiction.]*

**1** AN ACT regarding the jurisdiction of county courts to open roads in certain cases.

> Where new road begins and ends in same county—county or circuit of such county to have jurisdiction to establish, although proposed route may run through part of another county.

SEC. I. That whenever the route of a proposed new road commences in one county, and runs partly through another county, and finally terminates in that county in which it commenced, then, and in all such cases, the county court, or circuit court, of that county in which said road begins and terminates, shall have the right to appoint viewers to view the entire route in both counties; and shall have jurisdiction to open and establish the same, as now provided by law in cases where the road is wholly within the limits of one county.

SEC. II. This act to be in force from its passage.

**Trials.** SEC. VII. When the issues of fact in cases are made up for
trial, the clerk shall put the cases on the docket of the next trial
term, in proper order (cases in which several trials are allowed
by the Civil Code, and judgments against several defendants
may, if ready for trial as to some, and not ready against other,
proceed on both the trial and the appearance docket). And at
said trial terms issues of fact shall be tried, verdicts rendered,
motions for new trial, or in arrest of judgment, made and dis-
posed of, and final judgments on the pleadings may also be al-
lowed, and the pleadings may be amended to conform them to
the proof, or otherwise, upon just terms, in the discretion of the
court. All questions of law undisposed of, for want of time, or
under advisement, shall be continued to the next appearance
term. The appeals from justices' and other inferior courts shall
be docketed for the last days of the docket; and the first days
of the trial docket shall be set aside for county cases, according
to the present law concerning the Jefferson circuit court. Post-
ponements of trials shall be to the next trial term, or, if the
business of the court allow it, to a later day of the same term.

**Appeals.** SEC. VIII. Appeals from justices' and other inferior courts
shall go to the trial terms in the first instance, without regard
to the intervening appearance terms, and be otherwise regulated
by the laws regulating appeals to circuit courts; and when a
defendant appeals from a judgment for money or personal pro-
perty, he may demand judgment upon the two returns of not
found, if the plaintiff does not appear at the second term.

**Trial of appeals.** SEC. IX. Actions on mandamus and writs of prohibition may
be docketed for, or continued to either class of terms; and all
matters as to which the contrary is not above provided, which
are within the power of said court, may be done at either term;
and the rendering of any judgment, or allowing of any step, or
making an order at a term of the wrong class, shall never ren-
der a judgment void, nor shall it be error unless it be excepted
to at the proper time, and unless it moreover be of substantial
prejudice to the party complaining.

SEC. III. No divorce shall be granted under this act, except in cases where the lunacy or unsoundness of mind was the result of intemperance (or of an hereditary taint of insanity, which was concealed from the other party at the time of marriage), nor until the questions of fact arising under this act have been tried by a jury, as in cases of trials of issues of fact in equity cases: *Provided*, That in the event the jury returns an affirmative verdict, the divorce shall not be granted until and unless the chancellor shall enter upon the record that he has heard all the testimony, and that in his judgment the verdict is sustained by the evidence.

SEC. IV. Where the alleged lunatic, or person of unsound mind, is in an asylum established by the laws of this state, the case may be tried in the circuit or chancery court of the county where the asylum is located.

SEC. V. This act shall be in force from its passage.

1866.
*February 17.*

1 AN ACT in relation to the duties of county court clerks.

Duty of county court clerks to make and furnish poll-books.

SEC. I. That it shall be the duty of the several county court clerks of this commonwealth to make out and furnish to each clerk of the election, in their respective counties, a poll, at each election, for which they shall be allowed a reasonable compensation, to be allowed by the court of claims, and paid out of the county levy.

SEC. II. This act shall be in force from its passage.

All instruments that have been so acknowledged or proven before any clerk, and produced to, and recorded by, his successors, are hereby declared legally recorded, and such instruments and official copies therefrom, shall, to all intents and purposes, be taken and held precisely as if they had been lodged for record with the clerk before whom the acknowledgment or proof was made.

NOTE.—The section of the Revised Statutes amended by the above act will, as amended, read as follows:

"Where the office of any county court clerk has been, or may hereafter be vacated, leaving therein any instruments of writing unrecorded, which, from an official indorsement thereon, shall appear to have been acknowledged or proved ready for record, or that have been acknowledged or proved before another officer, and certified according to law, and lodged in his office for record, *or shall be produced to his successor for record*, the successor of such clerk shall record the same, making his certificate conform to the facts."

May 31.

*[Death of parties after judicial sale. Revivor unnecessary before conveyance.]*

AN ACT in relation to conveyances by commissioners.

SEC. I. That where, by the judgment of any of the courts of this commonwealth having jurisdiction thereof, a conveyance of real estate is ordered to be made by a commissioner or commissioners, and before the conveyance is perfected any of the parties die, it shall not be necessary to revive the action, but the conveyance, in pursuance of the judgment, shall be effectual to pass the title, notwithstanding the death of any of the parties.

SEC. II. That all conveyances heretofore made, in which the defect, supplied by the first section of this act exists, be, and they are hereby, made valid and effectual in law, for all pur-

8
LXXVI.] HUSBAND AND WIFE. 263

2. *When her choses in action subject to his debts.*—A chose in action accruing to the wife during coverture, which the husband can reduce into immediate possession, can be subjected to his debts in a proceeding against them as non-residents. *Tobin v. Dixon*, 2 Met. 422.

3. *Husband's right to deceased wife's slaves.*—By the Revised Statutes, and the previous law of 1846, "further to protect the rights of married women," the husband who survives the wife, is only entitled to a life estate in her slaves. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352; *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.

4. *Where slaves descended before law of 1846.*—Where the marriage took place in 1830, the husband, upon the death of the wife in 1857, was entitled to slaves by survivorship or as administrator, which had descended to her in 1829. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

5. *Right to deceased wife's choses in action.*—Husband surviving his wife is entitled to her choses in action, which right, on his decease, will pass to his executor. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

6. *Same.*—The wife having a vested right to a legacy not due at her decease, her husband is equitably entitled thereto, and may recover it when due. *Duncan v. Prentice*, 4 Met. 216.

7. *Sale of wife's slaves—how effected.*—The title to the wife's slaves can only be passed in the manner in which her lands may be sold and conveyed; and until so disposed of, her right to them is complete, subject only to the marital rights of her husband. *Tharp v. Tharp*, 3 Met. 372.

8. *Choses barred by husband's warranty.*—Where the right of the wife has been converted into a demand for money, to which the husband would be entitled when recovered, such demand will be barred by his covenant of warranty. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

9. *Husband's debt can not be set off against wife's rent.*—By the Revised Statutes it is declared that the rent and hire of the wife's real estate and slaves shall not be liable for any debt of the husband, nor shall his contingent right of curtesy or his right to the use or rent of his wife's estate be subjected to the payment of his debts during her life: *Held*—in an action by husband and wife to recover rent, for her property, that the tenant could not set off demands due him from the husband against the rent. *Green v. Carson*, 4 Met. 77.

## II. Necessaries. Selling and incumbering wife's ordinary estate.

10. *Necessaries, what are.*—The word necessaries as used in section one, article two, chapter forty-seven, Revised Statutes, should be liberally construed, and is held to embrace such things as the family, including the husband, considering its social position and the estate of the wife, ought to have and enjoy. *Pell v. Cole*, 2 Met. 252.

11. *Same.*—The wife owned eighty acres of land worth $1,500, with a house on it worth $1,275. This was all she had, and her husband was insolvent: *Held*—that a note given by her and her husband in payment of an addition put up to her house, was not for necessaries within the meaning of the law, and was not a lien on her property. *Ib.*

## 1860.
*March 3.*

*[Persons owning stone quarries near Ohio river may have road thereto.]*

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of persons holding lands lying back of other lands, in the vicinity of any of the navigable streams of this state," approved December 6, 1851.

SEC. I. That the provisions of the act, entitled "an act for the benefit of persons holding lands lying back of other lands, in the vicinity of any of the navigable streams of this state," approved December 6, 1851, be so amended as to embrace any person or persons owning or occupying a stone quarry within three miles of the Ohio river, or any navigable stream in this state.
2 Stanton's R. S. 296.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage

## 1861.
*December 10.*

*[Fines from delinquents to be paid to surveyor of highways. How expended.]*

AN ACT to amend the twenty-third section of chapter eighty-four, of Revised Statutes, concerning roads and passways.

SEC. I. That the fines that may be collected hereafter from delinquents in working on roads in this commonwealth, shall be paid to the surveyor of roads in the precinct in which the funds are collected, to be expended in the improvement of roads under his charge; and each surveyor of roads shall account to his successor in office for the funds so received by him, and pay over any unexpended balance remaining in his hands; and each surveyor shall report annually to the court of claims of his county the amount of fines received by him, if any, and the disposition thereof; and upon failure to do so, he shall be subject to a fine not exceeding ten dollars, to be recovered upon presentment of a grand jury.
How fines for failure to work on roads to be applied and accounted for.
Penalty.

SEC. II. The fine shall be paid by the person entitled to the services of slaves at the time they are required to work on a road, anything in said section to the contrary notwithstanding.

**SEC. XI.** In all actions brought originally in the Jefferson court of common pleas, or removed into it by appeal, in which the plaintiff claims damages for any trespass, libel, slander, illegal attachment or distress, breach of marriage promise, or on a bond given to obtain a provisional remedy in an action, and does, on such claim, recover no more than five dollars, the said court may, in its discretion, refuse to award the plaintiff the whole or any part of the costs.

**SEC. XII.** Said court shall have full power to make all proper rules to facilitate its business, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this state; and its judgments and final orders shall be subject to appeals, in like manner as those of circuit courts, and not otherwise.

**SEC. XIII.** In the summoning and selection of juries, and in the trial by jury, and the payment of jurors, in said court, the same shall be governed by the laws now in force, and which are applicable to circuit courts.

**SEC. XIV.** All laws or parts of laws within the purview of this act, and conflicting therewith, are repealed, from and after the taking effect of the parts of this act conflicting with them; and the provisions of this act shall take effect at the times indicated in the various sections.

## 1866.
*January 10.*

AN ACT to amend sub-division 6 of section 670 of the Civil Code of Practice, title "Evidence.

**SEC. I.** That sub-division 6 of section 670 of the Civil Code of Practice be, and the same is hereby, so amended as to allow the plaintiff's or owner's wife, in an action to recover the value of lost baggage against common carriers, inn-keepers, or wrong-doers, to testify as to the contents and value thereof, in the same manner and to the same extent that the husband is now allowed to testify by law.

Actions for lost baggage; plaintiff's wife made a competent witness.

**SEC. II.** This act shall take effect from its passage.

## 1866.
*February 13.*

AN ACT directing the purchase of the edition of the general laws of Kentucky prepared by Harvey Myers.

**SEC. I.** That the secretary of state be, and he is hereby, di-

XXVII.] CONVEYANCES. 115

7. *Unrecorded assignment—notice.*—An unrecorded assignment in behalf of creditors who have notice thereof before the acquisition of the legal title, is not avoided by the eleventh section of chapter twenty-four of the Revised Statutes. *Ward v. Crotty*, 4 Met. 59.

IV. *Construction and effect.*

8. *Prima facie validity of deed determined by laws of Kentucky.*—In the absence of an averment that a deed was made in another state, and proof of what the law of that state is, the validity of the deed must be tested by the law of Kentucky. *Hurdt v. Courtenay*, 4 Met. 139.

9. *Void conveyance—grantee not bound to complete transaction.*—A sale and conveyance of real estate by one executor was void, because the co-executor did not join therein: *Held*—that the purchaser was not bound by the transaction, and could not be compelled to accept the deed of the person to whom the land and its proceeds were devised. *Wells v. Lewis*, 4 Met. 269.

10. *What land passes by.*—A deed passes the title to all the land within the boundaries designated therein, although they may contain more than the quantity mentioned in the deed. *Jennings v. Monks*, 4 Met. 103.

11. *Title to whole boundary passes though greater than intended.*—A conveyance by a commissioner of land sold under judgment of court, described the premises by bounds, and as containing one hundred and seventy-four acres. It turned out that the tract sold and described in the deed contained two hundred and fourteen acres: *Held*—that the deed passed the title to the whole tract. *Ib.*

12. *The same—purchaser without notice.*—In the foregoing case, the purchaser of the land subsequently sold it to a third party who had no notice of the mistake as to the quantity and derived no benefit from it, and who paid for all the land: *Held*—that he could not be required to surrender the surplus or to pay for it. *Ib.*

13. *The word purchaser—construction of.*—The word purchaser has a well-defined technical meaning, and embraces every holder of the legal title to real or personal property acquired by deed. A mortgagee both before and after forfeiture, is a purchaser. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

V. *Voluntary conveyances.*

14. *Good against purchaser with notice.*—A voluntary conveyance is good against a subsequent purchaser with actual notice of its existence. *Earle v. Couch*, 3 Met. 450.

15. *Not always void as to subsequent creditors.*—Though a conveyance be voluntary, it is not on that account *alone* void as to subsequent creditors. *Hurdt v. Courtenay*, 4 Met. 140.

16. *Statute in favor of creditors to be liberally construed.*—The statutes to protect creditors against fraudulent conveyances, should be liberally con-

Digitized by Google
LXXII.] HUSBAND AND WIFE. 265

and distinctly proved that the gift or sale was made for the sole and separate use of the wife. *Tinsley v. Roll*, 2 Met. 509.

23. *Declarations of husband*.—The declarations of a husband that a slave belonged to his wife, which had been given to her previous to the act of 1846, do not even tend to prove that the wife has a separate estate in the slaves. *Ib.*

24. *Parol gift*—*intention of donor*.—Donor testifies that a slave was given to his married daughter as her own property, and that he intended the slave to be for her exclusive use and benefit. This did not create a separate estate. The *intention* of the donor can not create a separate estate, unless that intention be clearly and unequivocally *expressed* at the time of the gift. *Ib.*

25. *Language held to create separate estate*.—A deed provides that a trustee shall hold the property for the use and benefit and support of the wife and her children, and shall let her and her children have, use, and enjoy every part and parcel of the property, which is to be controled by her for her comfort and support, and for the support and education of her children: *Held*—to be a separate estate. *Toombs v. Stone*, 2 Met. 520.

26. *Trust estate and separate estate*.—The provisions of the Revised Statutes, concerning estates held in trust, have no application to separate estates which are something more than mere trust estates, and which can only belong to a married woman, though they may be created previous to her marriage. *Ib.*

27. *Separate estate not liable for necessaries*.—The provision of the Revised Statutes which renders the wife's real estate and slaves liable for necessaries, agreed to be paid for by writing signed by her and her husband has no application to her separate estate. *Ib.*

28. *Property conveyed on separation is separate estate*.—Where property is conveyed by a husband to a trustee for the benefit of his wife, and with a view to a separation between them, it is clear that a separate use was intended, and the trustees hold the property for her separate use. *Gaines v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

29. *What necessary to create separate estate*.—A stranger's conveyance of property, or covenant to pay money to a married woman or to a trustee for her, must, in order to create a separate estate, contain words indicating that intention. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 85.

30. *What necessary for husband to create separate estate*.—Where a wife joins with her husband in a sale of her property, in order to give him the proceeds, in consideration of which he gives her his notes, it is not necessary that the notes should specify that they are for her separate use in order to create a separate estate in her. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 85; *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

IV. *Selling and incumbering her separate estate.*

31. *Can not be sold or incumbered except, etc.*—The separate estate of a married woman, whether created before or since the Revised Statutes, can

## 1865.

*[Roads may be opened to oil wells and mines.]*

AN ACT to amend chapter eighty-four, section one, title "Roads and Passways," of the Revised Statutes.

**SEC. I.** That section one of the Revised Statutes, title "Roads and Passways," chapter eighty-four, be so amended as to authorize applications for roads to be opened to oil wells, copper mines, or other mines.
2 Stanton's R. S. 285.
Oil wells and mines.

## 1865.
*March 4.*

AN ACT requiring the militia to work on the state road.

**SEC. I.** That the militia residing within one mile of the state road leading from London to the Tennessee line, by way of Williamsburg, shall be liable to perform labor thereon, subject to the supervision of the commissioners respectively; *Provided*, That no one shall be required, under this act, to labor more than six days in any one year, subject to the laws now in force concerning county roads.
> Militia to work on state road.

**SEC. II.** This act shall be in force from and after its passage.

*June 3.*

*[Fine of $2 50 per day for failing to work on road. Notice, what sufficient and how proved.]*

AN ACT to amend sections twenty-three and twenty-four of chapter eighty-four, Revised Statutes, entitled "Roads and Passways."

**SEC. I.** That section twenty-three, chapter eighty-four, Revised Statutes, be so amended that hereafter the fine imposed by said section for failure or refusal to attend and labor, shall be two dollars and fifty cents for each day, instead of one dollar and twenty-five cents, and may be collected by warrant in the name of the commonwealth.
> 2 Stanton's R. S. 290.

IV. JEFFERSON LEVY AND COUNTY COURT.

1860.

February 25.

[Salary of judge.]

AN ACT to amend an act to establish a levy and county court for Jefferson county, approved February 25, 1854.

Sec. I. That the tenth section of an act, entitled, “an act to establish a levy and county court for Jefferson county,” be so amended as to give a discretion to the general council of the city of Louisville and the county court of Jefferson county to increase the salary of the judge of said court. But said increase shall not exceed in the aggregate four hundred dollars annually, and to be paid in like proportions as the salary of said judge is now paid by the city of Louisville and county of Jefferson.

Sec. II. That said increase shall not be made except by a majority of the justices of the peace in Jefferson county and the city of Louisville in session; and the judge of said court shall have no vote in making said order.

Sec. III. That said section be so amended as to allow the judge of said court to practice law in the Louisville chancery court.

Sec. IV. This act shall take effect from its passage.

2 Stanton's R.  
S. 523, 737.

County court and city council to have discretionary power to raise salary.

Majority of justices necessary.
EXECUTION. 715

after the first day of June, one thousand eight hundred and sixty-six, there shall be exempt from sale under execution, attachment, or judgment of any court, except to foreclose a mortgage given by the owner of a homestead, or for purchase money due therefor, so much land, including the dwelling house and appurtenances owned by the debtor, as shall not exceed in value one thousand dollars.

exempt from execution or attachment.

SEC. II. That before a sale of land under execution, order of attachment, or judgment of a court, the officer in whose hands the execution may be, or the court, shall cause so much thereof as shall not exceed in value one thousand dollars, and which may be selected by the defendant, to be valued under oath and set apart to him by two disinterested housekeepers of the county not related to either party; and, in case they disagree, the officer shall act as umpire.

How value to be ascertained.

SEC. III. That the valuation so made shall be in writing, signed by the persons making it, and returned to the court directing it, or with the execution; and the officer must refer to and explain the proceedings in his return on the execution.

Valuation to be in writing.

SEC. IV. That where the defendant in the execution, attachment, or action, owns real estate which is levied on or sought to be subjected to the payment of any debt or liability, and the same, in the opinion of the valuers, is of greater value than one thousand dollars, and is not divisible without great diminution of its value, then the same shall be sold under the execution, attachment, or judgment, and one thousand dollars of the money arising from the sale shall be paid to the defendant to enable him to purchase another homestead: *Provided, however*, That if the land, when offered for sale, does not bring more than one thousand dollars, there shall be no sale.

39. *Contracts upon separation, for support of wife.*—A contract by a husband, made in contemplation of the continuance of a previous separation from his wife, or of an immediate and continued separation, where disagreements have taken place between them, to support her or to convey property to a trustee for her benefit, is valid. *Gains v. Poor*, 3 Met. 503.

40. *Release of dower as a consideration.*—The release by the wife of her potential right of dower is a sufficient consideration to sustain a settlement upon her by her husband even against his creditors. *Ward v. Crotty*, 4 Met. 59.

41. *Agreement to release dower.*—An agreement by a husband to transfer to his wife a note for a part of the purchase money, for her separate use, in consideration of her release of her potential right of dower in land sold by him, is binding in equity, and may be specifically enforced. *Ib.*

42. *Agreement to release dower as a consideration.*—In consideration that the wife would release her potential right of dower in a tract of land which the husband desired to sell, he agreed to transfer to her one of the notes received in payment for the land: *Held*—that the agreement was binding, and could be enforced in equity. *Ib.*

43. *Same.*—In the case *supra* the husband delivered the note to the wife indorsed, "I assign the within note to C. W. (his wife), for satisfactory consideration." No trustee was named, nor was the assignment recorded: *Held*—that as against subsequent creditors, she is entitled to the note. As against prior creditors she is entitled to the value of her potential right of dower in the land. *Ib.*

44. *Antenuptial agreements.*—Executory agreements between husband and wife, made before their marriage, and to be performed during marriage, have frequently been enforced in equity, though void at law. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 84.

45. *Conveyance from husband to wife.*—Conveyances by husband to wife, without a trustee, are valid in equity, although at law the general rule is that both executed and executory contracts between them, without the intervention of a trustee, are void. *Ib.*

46. *When contract between husband and wife enforced.*—As a general rule whenever a contract would be good at law when made by the husband with trustees for the wife, that contract will be sustained in equity when made by a husband with his wife without the intervention of trustees, if it does not affect the rights of third persons. *Ib.*

47. *The same.*—The husband induced the wife to unite with him in selling her lands and slaves, he to use the proceeds in paying his debts, but upon an express promise that he would make her full compensation out of his own property. He also executed to her at the time his promissory notes for the amount received for the property: *Held*—that the agreement was valid in a court of equity and enforcible by the wife against the husband or his estate. *Ib.*

48. *Wife's equity as against creditors.*—But in the case *supra*, the husband having died insolvent: *Held*—that the wife's claim being purely

9. *Equity will interpose, when.*—Where a passway has been obstructed a court of equity will interfere on behalf of a person who has a right to use it, but it has no jurisdiction to establish a passway on the mere ground of necessity. *Ib.*

10. *What order awarding writ ad quod damnum must show.*—Upon an application to establish a private passway, the order awarding the writ *ad quod damnum*, must fix the day on which the inquest is to be held which must also be inserted in the writ, and the omission is fatal. *Troutman v. Barnes, 4 Met. 337.*

11. *Viewers of a road, competency of.*—That one of the viewers of a road was a brother-in-law of the plaintiff in the motion invalidates the report and is ground for quashing it. *Phillips v. Tucker, 3 Met. 69.*

12. *Report quashed—new appointment of viewers.*—Where the report of viewers is quashed, they can not make a second report without a new appointment. *Ib.*

13. *How route must be defined.*—The viewers of a road must fix its exact place of commencement and termination by some visible object. It is not sufficient that the beginning may be found by reversing the courses and distances run by the surveyor. A route to begin near the corner of P.'s orchard, where the turnpike makes a crook or bend, is insufficient. *Ib.*

14. *The same.*—The viewers of a road must describe the route laid out by them by metes and bounds, and courses and distances, and show the amount of land to be taken for the road. *Ib., and Craig v. North, Ib. 187.*

15. *The same.*—Such expressions as the following in defining the route of a road, to-wit: a "northwardly course," "following the general course of said creek northwardly," "crossing said creek *about* one hundred yards from C.'s line *near* the corner of N.'s land," are not sufficiently definite. *Craig v. North, 4 Met. 187.*

16. *Appeals in road cases.*—Any person has a right to resist an application for a gate across a public road, and upon doing so on the record, may prosecute an appeal from the order allowing its erection. *Bond v. Mullins, 3 Met. 282.*

17. *Order appointing viewers for gates, sufficiency of.*—The order appointing viewers on an application for the privilege of erecting gates across a road, must designate the precise places where it is proposed to erect them. "One at T. K.'s woodland pasture, and the other between said M.'s and Mrs. H.'s," is too indefinite and will render the order fatally defective. *Ib.*

18. *Report of viewers, sufficiency of.*—In the case *supra* the viewers reported that they had viewed "the places proposed to be gated by M. in this order:" *Held*—that the report was too indefinite, inasmuch as the places were not sufficiently defined in the order. *Ib.*

**THE REVISED STATUTES OF OHIO.**

**THE REVISED STATUTES OF THE STATE OF OHIO**, of a General Nature, in force August, 1, 1860. Collated by Hon. J. R. Swan. With Notes of the Decisions of the Supreme Court, and References to Prior Laws, by L. J. Critchfield.
2 Vols. Royal 8vo. $12.00

This work possesses the following merits:

I. The subjects are arranged in chapters alphabetically, with a synopsis at the head of each chapter of its contents.
II. Laws and sections which have been repealed are omitted, and their repeal noted.
III. When a new section has been enacted to take the place of a repealed section, the new section has been introduced into the place of the repealed section; printed in smaller type than the rest of the text, a marginal note placed by the side of it, stating that it is an amendatory section, and giving the time when it passed, and when it took effect. Former editions of the Statutes have contained no such arrangement.
IV. The Decisions of the Courts contained in the twenty-nine volumes of the Ohio Reports have been carefully collated and introduced as notes, accompanying the text, to illustrate its construction and subject matter.
V. It also contains, the Constitution of the United States, Abstract of Naturalization Laws, Old and New Constitutions of Ohio, with copious Indexes.

No care or expense has been spared to make the work perfect and reliable in all respects.
It has now the Legislative sanction, having been approved by nearly the unanimous vote of both Houses, and was ordered to be distributed to the following State and County Officers:
Governor, Attorney General, Supreme Judges, Secretary, Comptroller, Treasurer and Auditor of State, and to the Probate Courts, Courts of Common Pleas, Superior and Police Courts, Auditor and Clerks of the various Courts in each County, to the Members of the Senate and House of Representatives of this State, and the Governors of the several States of the Union.
This book, containing, as it does, all of the Statutes now in force, and the authoritative construction of them and of the New Constitution, will be found to be especially useful in the performance of their duties to all County Officers, Justices of the Peace, Township Trustees, Clerks of Townships, and City Officers.
Inasmuch as very many changes have been made in the Statutes since the publication of the last edition, by repeal, alterations and additions, and many important decisions have been given by the Supreme Court, on controverted points, all Attorneys at Law, Bankers, Merchants, and Business Men generally, will find this an invaluable work.

**NASH'S PLEADINGS AND PRACTICE.**

**PLEADING AND PRACTICE UNDER THE CIVIL CODES OF OHIO AND KANSAS.** By Hon. Simeon Nash. Third edition, thoroughly revised, enlarged and improved, to conform to Statutes in force January, 1864.

1 Vol. Royal 8vo. $6 00.

The Publishers would invite the attention of the Profession to their third and carefully revised and improved edition of this excellent and highly approved work on Pleading and Practice, under the New Code of Ohio, and that recently adopted in Kansas.
The work in its former editions received the approbation of the Profession, and has occupied a high stand as a careful analysis of the principles involved in the Code, and as a basis of uniformity and regularity in the forms and practice of Pleading throughout the State. The forms here supplied will be found convenient and concise, and adequate to nearly every case likely to arise under the requirements of the Code. They have already been much used in practice, and found admirably adapted to the purposes for which they are designed, conducing especially to uniformity, brevity and perspicuity—qualities in forms and proceedings at law which can not be too highly commended.
The Publishers would particularly present this work in the improved form, to the notice of the Profession in the States of *Indiana* and *Iowa*. The close resemblance of the Codes of these States to the one of which this work is a digest and practical analysis, renders it directly applicable to their practice. It will be found admirably suited to the practitioners of those States, and well calculated to aid them in arriving at that simplicity and uniformity in the Civil Practice, so desirable in every state and court in the country

**SENEY'S OHIO CODE.**

**THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, AND THE CODE OF PROCEDURE BEFORE JUSTICES OF THE PEACE, for the State of Ohio:** With Notes of the Decisions of the Courts of Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, and New York. By Hon. Geo. E. Seney, Judge of the Tenth Judicial District.

1 Vol. 8vo. $6.00.

This work contains the Code of Civil Procedure, and the Code of Procedure before Justices of the Peace, with all subsequent Amendments and Supplementary Acts, in force April, 1860; also, several Statutes of general application under the present system of Pleading and Practice. Brief notes of the published decisions of the courts of Ohio are appended to those sections of either Code, and to the Statutes referred to, to which a judicial construction has been given. Also, notes of the decisions of all the courts of Kentucky, Indiana and New York, upon all provisions of the Codes of either of those States, which are identical, or nearly so, with those of the Ohio Code.

These notes are compiled from the Ohio State Reports, Ohio Reports, Handy's Cincinnati Superior Court Reports, Cin. Law Gazette, Cleveland Law Monthly, Western Law Journal, Reports of the Court of Appeals of Kentucky, Supreme Court of Indiana, and the numerous Reports in New York.

In the arrangement of the work, where a section of either Code has been repealed and an amended section enacted to supply its place, the amended section is substituted for the repealed section, with a note stating the time when it was passed, and the volume of Laws in which it was published; also, giving the section repealed.

As no edition of the Code has been published since 1853, this volume, containing all the subsequent amendments and acts supplementary to the Code, with nearly three thousand notes of judicial decisions, construing its provisions, and determining many interesting and important questions of practice, will be found useful and convenient not only to the Bench and the Bar, but to Justices of the Peace.

*From Western Law Monthly.*

Whatever contributes to the dissemination of a correct knowledge of the Code of Civil Procedure of this State, and of the practice under it, is of great value to the whole people. We therefore hail with sincere satisfaction the work of Judge SENey. It bears the impress of the mature and cultivated legal mind and sound judgment characteristic of the author. The plan of the work does not admit of any discussion on his part. The object of the author has been to furnish a perfect copy of the present Code, with all the light that judicial decisions have thrown upon it or the practice under it; entirely divested of all speculations or opinions of his own. In this, he seems to have accomplished all that the present state of authorities admits; giving, at one view, all that is authoritative upon the subject, and saving to the practitioner and the court a vast amount of labor in research, that might in the end have proved less efficacious. The book should be deemed—as practically it is—indispensable to every lawyer, and every student of the Code. And it ought to be in the hands of every Justice of the Peace. It is supplied with a full table of contents, and a copious and minute index. It is printed and bound in the very best style of workmanship and material.

*From Hon. R. P. Ranney.*

I have carefully examined the work of Judge SENey upon the Ohio Code, and am much pleased with it. It is good for all it professes, and reflects credit upon both the author and publishers. The chief value of such a work must consist in the facility with which the practitioner can, in the hurry of business, refer to any disputed or doubtful question, and feel sure that he has seen all that can influence its decision. This is accomplished in this work by a very careful and judicious collection and arrangement of the various statutory provisions, and a reference to all the judicial decisions of several States in which the construction of similar statutes has been involved. The work is every thing that could be desired in this respect, and I think will be found a valuable aid to the bar of the State.

*From Hon. Milton Sutliff.*

From the cursory perusal of SENey's Ohio Code, which I have been able to give, I regard it as being all that its title page professes. It seems to be a work characterized by good judgment in its design, and much industry and care in its execution. The aid and facility thereby afforded for a correct understanding of the practice under the Code, I think, will insure the work a favorable reception by the legal profession.

*From Hon. James Murray, Attorney General of Ohio.*

After a very careful examination of the "Code of Civil Procedure of the State of Ohio, with Notes, etc., by GEORGE E. SENey," I have no hesitation in saying that I regard it as a most valuable aid to the legal profession. It is a work without which no lawyer's library is complete, and which should be in the hands not only of every lawyer in active practice, but also of every student of the law, as well as every Justice of the Peace in the State.

**XXXIII.]** **COUNTER-CLAIM AND SETOFF.** 129

8. *Money paid on a judgment afterward reversed.*—A party against whom a proceeding is instituted to compel a restitution of money collected on a judgment afterward reversed, can defeat a recovery by showing that the claimant (a non-resident) is indebted to him in a sum equal to the amount collected on the judgment. *Carson v. Carson*, 2 Met. 96.

9. *As between administrator and distributee.*—One who is a distributee of an estate, and who is sued by the personal representative for a debt due the latter in his own right, can not avail himself, by way of setoff, of the sum due him from the estate on final settlement, where the personal representative qualified and the settlement was made in another county than that in which the action is pending. *Bennett v. McCrocklin*, 3 Met. 322.

10. *Mutual debts; special agreement.*—Two persons mutually indebted to each other executed, each to the other, his note for the amount of his debt, with the understanding that the notes might be used to pay precedent debts, or for raising money by negotiation. One of the payees assigned his note and failed. In a suit by the assignee against the maker: *Held*—that the latter can not rely upon his note as a setoff against the note in the hands of the assignee. *Barbaroux v. Baker*, 4 Met. 47.

## II. Pleading and practice.

11. *Nature of a setoff.*—A cause of action used as a setoff is in the nature of an action, and is subject to all the restrictions imposed upon it, as though asserted by an original proceeding. *Bennett v. McCrocklin*, 3 Met. 322.

12. *In what county to be pleaded.*—Where a cause of action can only be asserted by original proceeding in a particular county, it is not maintainable in any other county as a setoff or counter-claim. *Ib.*

13. *Parties.*—Objections for want of necessary parties to an answer containing a setoff, must be taken in one of the modes prescribed by the Code; otherwise it is waived. *Taylor v. Stowell*, 4 Met. 175.

14. *Allegations of to be taken as true.*—Every material allegation of new matter in an answer, relating to a setoff therein relied on, where there is no reply, must be taken as true. *Ib.*

**See LIMITATIONS, Note 19.**

I.** That whenever there is no jail in any county, or the same is insecure, or there is danger that any person confined therein, under any order or process of the circuit court of the county; or where he is held to answer any charge in said court, will be rescued therefrom by violence, the judge of the circuit court for said county shall, by an order made of record, if said court is in session, or if the court is not in session, by his written order filed with the clerk of said court, direct that he shall be transferred to the jail of the nearest county where the jail is secure and it shall be deemed that he can be safely kept.

This outline of the Law of Partnership has been sketched for the use of the business community. It aims to give nothing more than a general view of the principles on which it is based, and the rules for their application. It is not designed to supersede the necessity of professional advice. **CURWEN ON ABSTRACTS OF TITLE.**

**A MANUAL OF ABSTRACTS OF TITLE TO REAL PROPERTY;** containing Practical Directions for the Examination of Titles to Real Property; Searching Records of Deeds, Wills, Judgments, Decrees, Mortgages, Liens; and the Doctrine of Equity with respect to Vendors' Liens, Separate Estates of Married Women, Notice of Trusts, etc. Illustrated by references to the Statutes of Pennsylvania, Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa and Kansas. By M. E. Curwen, of the Cincinnati Bar.

**1 Vol., 12mo. Cloth, $1.50. Law Sheep, $1.75.**

This work, believed to be the only one in the United States upon the subject, is designed to give plain, practical directions for the examination of records relating to the title to lands in the States of Pennsylvania, Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa and Kansas. The statutory requirements of these States in reference to contracts concerning lands, deeds, wills, leases, mortgages, liens, judicial sales, and the like, are fully stated, and the authorities referred to. The subject of oil lands and leases is considered, and the usual form of oil leases given.

Without going into the abstruse learning on the law of real property, the work states, sharply and definitely, the points of law to which the attention of men of property, real estate dealers, auctioneers and searchers of titles should be directed; the requisites of a binding contract of sale of land; the rules for computing the area of land; the requisites of deeds, leases, wills, and judicial titles; the liens which attach to lands; the provisions of the stamp act, and the usual forms of deeds and mortgages.

SEC. I. That real estate conveyed, or devised to a trustee, for the separate use of the present and any future wife of a certain person named in the instrument of conveyance, with remainder to the children of such wives, may, by the order of the circuit court of the county in which such land is situated, be sold by a commissioner appointed for that purpose by said court; and the money arising from the sale of such real estate shall be reinvested according to the order of such court, and under its superintendence, in other real estate, to be held for the same uses and trusts, and in the same manner in all respects as the property sold; but such order of sale shall only be made upon satisfactory proof to the court that the sale and reinvestment will be beneficial to all interested in the property, and upon a petition by the trustees, the present wife, and such children as are in being.

I. *What interests in real estate may be sold.*

1. *Remainders where part of remaindermen not in esse.*—Under the act of February, 1858, remainders where a part of the remaindermen may not yet be *in esse*, may be sold for the purpose of re-investment. *Bell v. Clark*, 2 Met. 573.

2. *What "real estate" comprehends.*—The words "real estate," as used in article three, chapter eighty-six, of the Revised Statutes, imports lands, tenements, and hereditaments, and all rights thereto, and interests therein, other than a chattel interest. *Nutter v. Russel*, 3 Met. 163.

# 1861.
*May 24.*

AN ACT to suspend the circuit and other courts in this commonwealth, and for other purposes.*

**SEC. I.** That all laws requiring circuit courts, equity and criminal courts, quarterly courts, justices' courts, and all police, town, and city courts, except for the trial of criminal and penal causes in this commonwealth, be, and they are hereby, repealed until the first day of January, 1862: *Provided*, That after the expiration of said time, said courts shall be held and governed by all the laws now in force: *And provided further*, That all civil process of every kind, returnable to said terms, shall be continued until the next regular terms of said courts after the first day of January, 1862; and nothing herein contained shall be construed so as to interfere in any manner whatever with the trial of criminal and penal causes in said courts at their regular terms, as though this act had not taken effect: *And provided further*, That the circuit court of Boyle county be held at its regular August term for the trial of all contested cases remaining on the docket of said court at the February term, 1861. That the several judges of the equity and circuit courts shall hold the terms of their several courts at the times now fixed by law for the trial of criminal and penal prosecutions, and for the purpose of the assignment of dower, for the trial of cases of divorce, for the probate of wills, for making the partition of land, ordering the distribution of estates ratably among the creditors, and for the trial of actions of tort, actions of forcible entry and detainer, and forcible detainer, and cases where the title to land or other property is in dispute, or where the boundary of land is involved, or a party is seeking to establish or complete, by judgment or order of court, title to lands, and all causes between principal and agent, between trustees and *cestui que trust*, and for all other cases of law or equity where a decree or judgment for money is not to be rendered, and for the taking of all proper steps for the preparation of cases in said courts.

Laws requiring circuit courts, eq. and crim. courts, quarterly courts, justices' courts, police, town and city courts, repealed until 1st January, 1862.

SEC. II. He may exchange with any state in this Union copies of the school reports and documents of this state, for copies of the school reports and documents of such other state.

SEC. III. Copies of records and papers in his office, certified by him, shall, in all cases, be evidence equally with the originals.

SEC. IV. He shall keep his office in the city of Frankfort, or in some city or town within a day's travel therefrom.

SEC. V. The school commissioners of the several counties of this commonwealth shall, so soon as the school fund coming to their respective counties shall have been received by them, and the proper demand made, pay out and disburse the same to the persons entitled thereto, and within ninety days settle their accounts with the county court, and forward a copy of such settlement, certified by the clerk of said court, to the superintendent of public instruction, and said copies of settlement with the county court shall embrace all sums received since the date of their last settlement, by said commissioners for the benefit of common schools, and also contain a full statement of all sums paid out by them, for what, to whom, and when paid; and should any commissioner fail to pay out and disburse the common school moneys received by him, and to make settlement and report the same within ninety days, as above directed, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, on indictment in the circuit court, be fined in a sum not less than one hundred, nor more than five hundred dollars, as well as remain liable on his official bond.

SEC. VI. Any school commissioner who shall knowingly and willfully report to the superintendent of public instruction a number of common schools as having been taught in his county greater than the number of such schools actually taught therein according to law, or a number of children entitled to tuition in his county greater than the actual number of such children therein, or otherwise knowingly and willfully misstate any fact or facts which he now is, or may hereafter be required by law to report to said superintendent, shall be deemed guilty of a high misdemeanor, and shall, on conviction thereof, on indictment in the circuit court, be fined in a sum not less than two hundred, nor more than five hundred dollars, or imprisoned in the county jail not longer than six months, or both so fined and imprisoned, in the discretion of the jury.

and all executions hereafter issued in this commonwealth by the officers authorized to issue the same, shall be made returnable on the first day of March, 1862; and no officer, by authority of any execution issued under this act, shall sell any personal property of any kind until he has caused said property to be appraised, as land is now required to be appraised, unless such property shall at such sale bring at least two thirds of its appraised value; and all sales of such property, which shall be made under any such executions for less than two thirds of its appraised value, shall be null and void, and invest the purchaser with no title to the same: but said execution shall still hold and retain a lien on said property: *Provided*, That any lien which may have been acquired or created by the issuing of an execution, shall not be released or impaired by the return of said execution, but shall continue in full force on any subsequent execution that may be issued; and after the said first of March, 1862, executions shall be issued and regulated according to the laws now in force, and sales of property after that time shall also be regulated by the laws in force previous to the passage of this act: *And provided further*, That this act shall not release the liability or responsibility of any assignor or assignors to his or their assignee or assignees on any assignment or transfer on any note, bond, or contract, or any surety on any note or contract on which suit has not been instituted, or on which judgment has been rendered: *Provided*, This act shall not be so construed as to prohibit any person whatever from bringing suits in any of the courts aforesaid, and obtaining any provisional remedy under existing laws, nor shall the law, as it now stands, authorizing the trial of attachments and other provisional remedies, be in any wise obstructed by the provisions of this act; and judges and justices of the peace of said courts shall have power, under existing laws, to make any necessary order for the sale or preservation of attached property, or to enter any order authorized by the present laws for the distribution of any money whatever among the parties entitled to receive the same, in any case pending in any of said courts.

1. *Grand jurors only to be summoned by sheriff or coroner.*—The sheriff, or where there is no sheriff, the coroner, only is entitled to summons bystanders to sit upon a grand jury. If bystanders be summoned by another person, though appointed by the court, an indictment found by such jury may be quashed on motion. *Commonwealth v. Graddy, 4 Met. 223.*

2. *The same.*—The provision of the Criminal Code authorizing the court to designate some other person to summons jurors, relates only to petit juries. *Ib.*

3. *Who may summons when selected by commissioners.*—Where jury commissioners have selected grand jurors as directed by the Revised Statutes, the court incline to the opinion that the summoning them by a person other than the sheriff or coroner, would not be a "substantial error." *Ib.*

SEC. I. That hereafter, in actions for the recovery of money or property, where the amount claimed by the plaintiff does not exceed fifty dollars, exclusive of interest, and the defendant or defendants are non-residents of Kentucky, and said defendants are the owners of real estate in this commonwealth, but not of personal estate sufficient to satisfy the demand of the plaintiff, jurisdiction is hereby conferred upon the circuit and equity and criminal courts of the county where such real estate is situate to try such action, as though the amount claimed exceeded fifty dollars; and the plaintiff shall be entitled to sue out all such provisional remedies, and to prosecute his demand in the same way and under the same rules as now apply to similar actions in said courts, where jurisdiction is now given by law.

# TITLE LXXXI.
## LANDLORD AND TENANT.

1. *Extent of landlord's lien.*—If the landlord (prior to the act of February, 1858) claim the lien conferred by the fourteenth section of article two, Title Landlord and Tenant, he can only obtain it by having a distress warrant issued and levied on the property. Otherwise he will be entitled only to the provision made for him by the twentieth section, by which the officer levying an execution or attachment on the tenant's property is only required to pay the landlord out of the proceeds thereof, such rent as is due and in arrear, not exceeding one year's rent. *Williams v. Wood*, 2 Met. 41.

2. *Same under act of February 16, 1858.*—By the act of February 16, 1858, an exclusive lien is given to the landlord on all the personal property of the tenant found on the leased premises after possession taken by the tenant, and this lien exists and may be enforced by the landlord without suing out a warrant to enforce it. *Ib.*

3. *How distress warrant discharged.*—The law prescribes but one mode in which the levy of a distress warrant may be discharged, and that is by executing the bond prescribed by section seven hundred and twenty-one of the Civil Code. *Grubbs v. McCoy*, 2 Met. 486.

4. *Invalid bond.*—Tenant whose property had been levied under a distress warrant, executed a bond to the effect that if the property should be adjudged subject to the distress warrant, he would pay to the plaintiff the value thereof, and ten per cent. interest, and thereupon the property was returned to the tenant: **Held**—That the bond was unauthorized and could not be enforced by motion: that it did not discharge the levy, and that the officer might peaceably retake possession of the property and sell it, although he had returned the warrant. *Ib.*

5. *Tenant executing bond, admits tenancy.*—If the defendant in a distress warrant executes the bond authorized by section seven hundred and twenty-one, Civil Code, he thereby admits that he is either tenant, assignee, or sub-tenant, and on trial of the motion for judgment on the bond is estopped to deny that character, or to assert that he was but a surety of the lessee, or that the lease was procured by fraud or mistake. *Pegard v. Kellar*, 4 Met. 260.

6. *Relief in equity.*—Whether in the case *supra* the defendant might not be relieved in equity, is not decided. *Ib.*

7. *Attachment for rent must conform to the Code.*—The provisions of chapter fifty-six, article two, section six, Revised Statutes, providing that upon the return into the circuit court of an attachment issued by a justice

# 1860.

AN ACT in relation to donations, devises, and gifts to the common school fund of Kentucky.

**SEC. I.** That when the auditor of public accounts shall be informed that any donation, gift, or devise of any real or personal estate shall have been made to the common school fund of Kentucky, it shall be the duty of the auditor to appoint some discreet person, who shall take charge of the personal or real estate so granted, devised, or donated, and sell and dispose of the same, and pay the proceeds into the treasury of Kentucky. Before said agent, so appointed by the auditor, shall proceed to act, he shall give a covenant, with good security, to the commonwealth, for the faithful discharge of his duties under this act. Said covenant shall be given in the county in which the donor, grantor, or devisor shall have died, or in the county in which the property is situated. The covenant to be executed in, and approved by, the county court.

> How donations, gifts, and devises to comm'n school fund to be secur'd

**SEC. II.** The person so appointed shall make a settlement with the county court of his county once in each year, and shall pay into the treasury the amount found in his hands after said settlement. The court shall allow said person a reasonable compensation for collecting and paying over said money, which amount said person shall retain in his hands out of the money collected.

Agent to settle and pay.
May retain compensation in his hands.

**SEC. III.** For any failure of the person so appointed to discharge the duties under this act, he and his securities shall be liable to all damages sustained, and for all money collected, with twenty per cent. damages on the amount so collected, and which he has failed to pay over. The suit on the covenant shall be in the name of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, and shall be instituted by the commonwealth's attorney.

> Agent and sureties liable on their bond.

**SEC. IV.** The person appointed by the auditor shall have the same power in collecting and settling the estate as an administrator or executor now has by law; may institute and defend all suits in reference to said estate, sue, and collect all notes, bonds, etc., and sell and convey the real estate by deed.

> Power given to agent.

# PREFACE.

THIS volume embraces the GENERAL LAWS OF KENTUCKY, enacted by its Legislature since the publication of MR. STANTON'S edition of the REVISED STATUTES, in 1859. The propriety of bringing those laws together in one volume, under appropriate titles, will doubtless be admitted by all who are conversant with the subject. Whether this attempt to perform that task has been attended with a reasonable degree of success must be determined by those for whose use the volume is intended.

I have aimed at publishing in the volume all laws of a *general character* and of a *permanent nature*. In theory, it is easy to define the boundary between general, and local or private laws; and between laws of a permanent, and those of a temporary nature; but in applying the theory to the legislation of the last six years, it has often been difficult to decide whether a law was of a sufficiently general nature, or of a sufficiently permanent character, to justify its publication. Whenever there has been any great doubt on the subject, I have published the act, feeling that its publication would be attended with less inconvenience than might happen if it were omitted.

Several laws passed with reference to the civil war through which we have just passed were, either in express terms, or from the nature of their subject-matter, limited to the period of that conflict. Others, though passed with reference to it, would remain permanent laws until repealed by the legislature. They were mostly arranged for the press before the termination of hostilities, and when it could not be foreseen how long the conflict might continue. Some of them might have been properly omitted, after the

PREFACE.

war had virtually ceased, and I at first determined to drop them;
but, upon reflection, they seemed to me to possess a historical and
political interest which rendered their publication proper, even
though several of them had expired, and others should be imme-
diately repealed by the legislature. I have accordingly retained
them. They occupy but a few pages of the book.

The fees and salaries of the state and county officers were gener-
ally raised by acts of the last legislature—the acts, in some in-
stances, being limited to two or three years duration, and in others
being without limitation. These acts were of immediate and prac-
tical importance to those for whose benefit they were passed, and,
while in force, must be constantly before the courts and officers of
the state and counties. To omit them, it seemed to me, would have
been to have left the volume defective, and impaired its usefulness
for a number of years to come. I have, therefore, published them
also.

I have followed, as nearly as the subject-matter would admit,
those titles laid down in the REVISED STATUTES, even where they
did not seem to be exactly applicable, because the people of the
state were familiar with those titles, and would be guided by them
in searching any volume of the statute laws of the state. But in
many instances other titles had to be interposed, and in others, sub-
jects which were arranged as subdivisions of some general title of
the REVISED STATUTES, I have treated as independent titles; a
volume of this character not seeming to admit of general titles,
with subdivisions. Thus the subject of DIVORCE and DOWER, which
are subdivisions of the general title of HUSBAND AND WIFE in the
REVISED STATUTES, I have treated as independent titles; and so in
other cases.

Many acts could, with propriety enough, be placed under two or
more titles; in other instances, portions of an act were appropriate
subjects for one title, while other portions fell properly under some
other title. In these cases I have placed the act entire under one
title, and inserted the other title (where it was not already in) at its
proper place, with a reference to the title where the act could be

PREFACE. v

found. To facilitate these references, I have, where there is more than one act under a title, numbered them in the margin opposite the title of the act, and the references are to those numbers.

In arranging the acts, I have generally followed the chronological order in which they were passed, but in several instances have departed from this order, where perspicuity and the subject-matter of the acts seemed to call for that departure.

At the head of each act I have placed the date of its becoming a law; and where the title of the act did not indicate the general scope of its contents, I have placed in brackets a short note indicating the character and object of the law.

I found a multitude of acts changing and modifying the time of holding the CIRCUIT, EQUITY, AND CRIMINAL courts of the state, and enlarging or diminishing the length of the terms. To have published them as they were would have presented a chaotic and disorderly mass of acts of little practical use to any body. I have, therefore, undertaken to extract from them a concise statement of the TIME of holding those courts, and the LENGTH OF THE TERMS, in each county of the state, referring to the acts by which I was guided, which, I trust, will be found correct, and more acceptable than a publication of the numerous acts from which the statement is abstracted.

I have published the General Laws applicable to Jefferson County only, at the end of the volume, arranging them under such subdivisions as seemed appropriate.

MR. STANTON, in his edition of the Statutes, brought down the notes of the decisions of the Court of Appeals, construing the statute laws of the state to the second volume of METCALFE'S REPORTS. I have continued those notes in this volume down to and including the decisions in fourth METCALFE. MR. # 1864.
*January 30.*

AN ACT to revise, amend, and reduce into one the common school laws of Kentucky.

## ARTICLE I.
### SCHOOL FUND.

**SEC. I.** That the school fund is the fund dedicated by the constitution and the laws of this commonwealth to sustaining a system of common schools therein.

**SEC. II.** The auditor shall keep the accounts in relation to this fund. He shall, once in each month, make a transfer to the credit of said fund of all receipts into the treasury for the benefit of the common schools of the state, up to the date of such transfer. He shall allow no expenditures on that account beyond the annual revenue of the fund; and see that no county draws more than its proper proportion.

**SEC. III.** Annually, as soon after the fifteenth day of February as practicable, he shall apportion the revenue of the fund among the several counties of the state, according to the number of free white children in each between the ages of six and twenty years, as shown by the returns of the assessors in his office, or the reports of the school commissioners to the superintendent of public instruction, whichever is the greater in each county. If in any year the returns be not in by the fifteenth day of February, he shall use the returns of the last preceding year. He shall, at the same time, apportion the share of each county among the several school districts thereof in which a common school shall have been actually taught in that year, according to law, in proportion to the number of free white children in each district between the ages of six and twenty years, as shown by the report of the school commissioner to the superintendent of public instruction for that year, and, if no such report be in, then as shown by the report of that officer for the last preceding year.

440 SCHOOLS AND SEMINARIES. [TITLE

lishing or sustaining any school or seminary other than common schools, in each county.

**Revenue to be paid into treas'ry**
SEC. V. The revenue of the school fund, and the proceeds of all taxes levied in aid thereof, together with all fines and forfeitures inflicted or granted for the benefit of the common schools of the state, shall be paid into the treasury, but shall never be drawn out or appropriated otherwise than pursuant to this chapter in aid of common schools.

**Balances due the counties to be transferred and bond given therefor.**
SEC. VI. On or before the first day of July, 1864, the board of education shall transfer to the commissioners of the sinking fund the surplus due the several counties of the state, for the several years intervening between the date of the last transfer of surplus and the aforementioned first day of July, 1864, and to this sum shall be added the surplus heretofore transferred, and for which bonds were issued by the governor, bearing date, respectively, August the twenty-third, 1855, and February the twenty-third, 1857; these bonds canceled, and one new bond issued by the governor for the whole amount of surplus due the counties for common school purposes, with a detailed statement of the amount due each county. In canceling the bonds referred to, dated, respectively, August the twenty-third, 1855, and February the twenty-third, 1857, the interest which shall have accrued, and remained unpaid, shall be set apart and credited to the counties of the state, entitled to draw it, and at the next regular apportionment and distribution of the revenue of the school fund of the state to the several counties thereof, shall be added to the distributable share of the counties of the state, entitled to draw it, and paid out at the same time and in the same way in which said distributable share is paid out. After the cancellation of these bonds, there shall never be more than one bond in existence at one and the same time, for surplus due the counties; but, annually, thereafter, on the same day of the same month on which this first cancellation takes place, a new transfer of surplus shall be made by the board of education to the commissioners of the sinking fund, the existing bond canceled, and a new bond issued for the whole amount of balances due the counties, with a detailed statement as heretofore mentioned.

**Thereafter only one bond for surplus due counties**

**Conditions of bond.**
SEC. VII. This bond shall not be transferable, shall be redeemable at the pleasure of the legislature, and shall bear six per cent. interest.

applies to any other subject than that embraced in the title was inoperative and void. *Chiles v. Monroe*, 4 Met. 72.

3. *Void unless reasonable time allowed to sue.*—Statutes of limitation which do not allow a reasonable time after their passage in which to commence suits upon existing causes of action impair the obligation of contracts and are void. *Berry v. Randall*, 4 Met. 292.

4. *Thirty days not reasonable.*—The act of March 15, 1862, by which, after thirty days, the limitations contained in chapter sixty-three of the Revised statutes should extend to all cases, irrespective of when the right of action accrued, is unconstitutional and void. *Ib.*

## II. What cases they embrace.

5. *Actions pending when statute passed.*—The act of February 4, 1858, entitled "an act to amend the second section of article sixty-three of Revised Statutes, entitled 'limitation of actions and suits,'" does not embrace causes of action on which suits were pending at the time the act was passed. *Hedger v. Rennaker*, 3 Met. 255.

6. *Bar not complete when action brought.*—By chapter sixty-three, Revised Statutes, actions on notes are barred after fifteen years, except where the right accrued prior to the adoption of the statute. An amendatory act of February 4, 1858, extended the provisions of that chapter to all cases whether the right of action accrued before or after the Revised Statutes took effect, from and after the first of August, 1859. A suit brought before the last named day on a note due more than fifteen years before the commencement of the action, is not embraced by the law. *Fenwick v. Phillips*, 3 Met. 87.

7. *What cases embraced by act of 1858.*—The act of 1858, *supra*, was only intended to apply to such actions as were brought after the first of August, 1859. *Ib.*

## III. When and against whom the statute runs.

8. *Between tenant for years and remainderman.*—Where a slave was given to a married woman until her son attained to the age of twenty-one years, and then to the son, and the son permitted his mother and her husband to hold the slave more than five years after he was entitled to it: *Held*—that his right of action was barred. *Divine v. Bullock*, 3 Met. 418.

9. *Remainderman not barred by adverse possession during particular estate.*—The statute never runs against a party until his right of action accrues. Hence adverse possession during the continuance of a life estate forms no bar to the right of the remainderman. *Williams v. McClanahan*, 3 Met. 420.

10. *The same.*—Property was devised to one in fee, but to go to another in case the devisee died without issue. Pow'r to organize county teachers' association.
SEC. VII. A commissioner shall have the power to organize a county teachers' association, of which he shall be, *ex officio*, president, and of which all the trustees and teachers of the common schools of the county, together with any other teachers of the county, whom he or the association may invite to participate in the proceedings thereof, shall be members. The county association shall do nothing inconsistent with the regulations which the board of education may prescribe.

When and where to receive reports from trustees.
SEC. VIII. Each commissioner shall attend at the county seat on the first Thursday, Friday, and Saturday in January, in each year, to receive reports from the trustees of the common schools of his county, and before the 15th day of January, he shall prepare and mail, and before the 15th day of February, must cause to be placed in the hands of the superintendent of public instruction, the report of said commissioner. Said report shall embrace the following items, to-wit: *First*, The districts in which common schools have been reported to him as having been regularly taught for at least three months during the year, and which he believes to have been so taught. *Second*, The whole number of free white children between the ages of six and twenty years, residing in each district, on the 10th day of January preceding the report. *Third*, The whole time a common school has been kept in each district during the year by a qualified teacher. *Fourth*, The highest, lowest, and average number of children in attendance during that time. *Fifth*, The cost of tuition for each child for three months, according to the highest number in attendance at any one time. *Sixth*, The amount of money received from the state the preceding year, and how distributed; and the disposition of the surplus, if any.

Sureties. 11. *Forbearance induced by sureties.*—That the payee in a note was induced by the sureties to indulge them (the principal being insolvent), and the indulgence was thus extended beyond seven years, is not a defeating or obstructing suit upon the note within the meaning of the act of 1838, and can not therefore deprive the sureties of the right to rely upon that act in bar of the action. *Coleman v. Walker, 3 Met. 65.* 12. *"Defeat and obstruct"*—how construed.—The words defeat and obstruct as used in the act *supra* signifies such conduct on the part of the sureties as amounts to a hindrance or prevention of suit in opposition to the will of the creditor, and which he can not overcome by reasonable diligence. *Ib.* 13. *Liability incurred before Revised Statutes.*—Where the surety of an administrator incurred a liability before the Revised Statutes took effect, the provisions of these statutes do not apply. *Hayden v. Hayden, 4 Met. 189.* 14. *Infancy of distributees.*—To enable a surety in an administrator's or guardian's bond to rely upon the limitation prescribed in the second section of the act of 1838, he must aver and prove that five years had elapsed *after the youngest* of the distributees, devisees or wards had attained majority before suit was brought. *Ib.* 15. *Party held to be principal and not surety.*—Upon the separation of husband and wife a third party entered into an obligation indemnifying the husband against any claim of the wife upon him for alimony or dower: *Held*—that the obligor was not the surety of the wife but a principal, and that he could not rely upon the seven year statute in favor of sureties. *Gaines v. Poor, 4 Met. 503.* 16. *The same.*—A dower slave was sold by a doweress, and the slave removed from the state. The price was laid out by the doweress in other slaves: *Held*—that the reversioner's cause of action to recover the slaves so purchased, did not accrue until the death of the doweress, the forfeiture of the life estate having been waived. *Williams v. McClanahan, 3 Met. 420.* 17. *Actions to recover usury.*—Where the borrower pays the usury to the lender, who on the same day re-loans the principal and usury to him, in accordance with an agreement to that effect, the statute against the recovery of the usury begins to run from the day of such payment. *Hays v.
