1. *Power of legislature to suspend courts*.—Section fifteen, of article thirteen, of the state constitution relates altogether to the judicial and not to the legislative department of government, and lays down general rules as to the manner of conducting business by courts. It was not designed to regulate either the jurisdiction or terms of court. *Johnson v. Higgins*, 3 Met. 566.

2. *Remedy no part of contract*.—Laws prescribing the terms and jurisdiction of courts, do not relate to the *remedy* for enforcing the contract, but to the tribunal by which the remedy is to be enforced. In a legal sense, the courts compose no part of the remedy. *Ib*.

452 SCHOOLS AND SEMINARIES. [TITLE

**How change location.** The trustees may, with the assent of a majority of those voting at an annual election, change the location of the school house, sell the old site, and use the proceeds toward procuring a new one, or, if the terms of any gift shall so require, the old site shall revert to the donor, or the question may be determined by a vote taken at any other time, ten days' notice of the time and place of voting having first been given by advertisements posted at three public places in the district.

**To select teachers** SEC. VII. They shall have power to select a qualified teacher, agree with him as to compensation, and for good cause, of which he shall first be notified in writing, remove him.

**May appoint collector.** SEC. VIII. They may appoint a collector to collect all sums due the teacher by subscription or otherwise, and allow him a reasonable compensation therefor.

**Shall invite all indigent children to attend school.** SEC. IX. It shall be the duty of the trustees to invite and encourage all the indigent children in the district to attend the school, and to inform them and their parents that such is their right, for which the state pays, though they themselves may contribute nothing toward paying the expense of the school. The annual report of the trustees shall always show that this duty has been performed; and no arrangement shall be made for the benefit of some individuals of this description to the exclusion of any others.

**Shall visit the school weekly.** SEC. X. One of the trustees shall visit the school at least once in each week; see that the regulations for its government are complied with, and that the teacher performs his duty. They shall perform this duty in rotation, or otherwise as may be agreed upon. Upon complaint of the teacher, they shall have power to expel a scholar.

**Shall report to commiss'r under oath, items, to-wit** SEC. XI. Immediately after the termination of the school year, to-wit, immediately after the 31st of December, each year, the trustees shall report to the county commissioner, under the oath of any two of them, or of one of them, provided he shall bring a certificate from both or either of the others that he is authorized to act for them, as follows, to-wit:

1. The number of free white children between the ages of six and twenty, residing in their district.
2. The whole time that a common school has been kept in the district during the year, by a qualified teacher.
3. The highest, lowest, and average number of children in attendance during that time.

Defective sales of infant's real estate, how remedied.

What facts to be averred.
Judgment.
SEC. I. That in all cases wherein a judgment or decree for the sale of an infant's real estate has been rendered upon the petition of the guardian of such infant, and a sale has been made under such judgment or decree, and it shall appear that there are such errors or defects in the proceedings in which the judgment or decree was obtained as will or may vitiate the same, it shall be lawful for any purchaser of the estate, under such judgment or decree, to file his petition in the court rendering the same, making the infant and all other persons interested parties, reciting the facts of such proceeding, judgment, or decree, and sale, and averring that the sale was fairly made, and at the reasonable value of the property, that the sale was confirmed and the price paid by the purchaser, and that the sale was at the time beneficial to the infant, which petition shall be sworn to by the purchaser.

SEC. II. Upon the filing of such petition, and the parties being regularly before the court, it shall proceed to hear proof; and, if satisfied of the truth of the allegations, it shall render a judgment or decree confirming the original judgment or decree and sale, which shall render the same as valid and binding in all respects as if the original proceedings had been in every particular according to the requirements of the law.

SEC. III. This act shall take effect from its passage, and continue in force three years thereafter.

1. *Upon married woman's estate*.—The husband can not create a mechanic's lien upon the estate of his wife. *Pell v. Cole*, 2 Met. 252.

2. *The same*.—An action was brought to enforce a mechanic's lien upon the property of the wife, for buildings erected thereon, and for which she and her husband had executed a note. It did not appear that she procured the erection of the building, or that it was essential to the comfort or shelter of the family: *Held*—that no lien existed. *Ib*.

3. *Lien favored by the law*.—Debts owing to mechanics, for the construction or repair of houses in the city of Louisville, are favored by the law, and a preference is given to them over other debts to the extent of the value of the property improved, which had not been incumbered. *Brown v. Story*, 4 Met. 316.

4. *Mechanic's lien an incumbrance*.—A mechanic's lien upon a house constructed by him in the city of Louisville for the amount due him therefor, is an incumbrance within the meaning of chapter thirty-six, article fifteen, Revised Statutes. *Ib*.

5. *Levy upon property subject to this lien*.—Where property subject to a mechanic's lien is levied on and sold under execution, the purchaser only acquires a lien on the property for the purchase money and interest after the rate of ten per cent. per annum from the date of the sale till paid. *Ib*.

6. *The same*—*removing the lien*.—That the property was not levied on or sold as incumbered property, and, that after the sale, the purchaser removed the incumbrance, will not give him an absolute title. *Ib*.

7. *Other creditors*—*rights of*.—Other creditors may, before the purchaser has, *by suit*, removed the incumbrance, bring suit to subject the incumbered property, as authorized by section two, article fifteen, chapter thirty-six, Revised Statutes. *Ib*.

8. *Same*—*incumbrance removed without suit*.—But where the purchaser under the execution removes the mechanic's lien without suit, and sells the property before the creditors sue, the sale will stand, and a personal judgment be awarded against the party for whatever he has received above his debt and interest. *Ib*.

How deserters may be arrested.

SEC. VI. Should any of the civil officers enumerated in the preceding section, refuse to issue such warrant, or make such arrest and delivery, the officer and his sureties, so offending, shall pay to the Commonwealth of Kentucky not less than fifty nor more than three hundred dollars, to be recovered by civil action, in the circuit court of the county in which such officer lives; and one half of the amount so recovered shall be for the benefit of any citizen of this commonwealth who may institute and prosecute an action for the recovery thereof; and beyond such recovery by civil action, any officer so offending may be removed from his said office by the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction.

Penalty on civil officers for failing in their duty.

SEC. VII. Any person who entices, persuades, or attempts to induce, any officer or soldier of the State of Kentucky, mustered into the service of said state, or into the service of the United States, to desert such service, or harbors, conceals, or aids in the escape of any deserter from either service, is declared guilty of a high misdemeanor, and shall pay to the Commonwealth of Kentucky not less than fifty nor more than three hundred dollars, to be recovered by the same means and for the same purposes as declared in the last section, or by indictment.

Penalty on those who entice, persuade, or harbor deserters.

SEC. VIII. This act to take effect and be in force from and after its passage.

### 1862.

AN ACT creating the office of corresponding secretary of state.* 1

SEC. I. That the governor of this commonwealth, be and he is hereby authorized, for the time being, to appoint an additional secretary of state in the executive department, to be styled the "Corresponding Secretary," if in the opinion of the governor the labors of that department require it; and to be discontinued by the governor whenever the services of said office can be dispensed with.

SEC. II. Said officer shall perform such duties as may be required by the governor, and shall, during the continuance of the office, receive out of the treasury an annual salary of one thousand dollars, to be paid to him as the salaries of other public officers are paid.

314 MILITARY. [TITLE

number of volunteers furnished, and the remainder of its quota raised by draft. In making out the rolls of the volunteer and drafted companies and regiments, they shall be so made out as to show what county furnishes each volunteer and drafted man.

**Exemptions—how proved.**
SEC. VI. Notice shall be published in each county, in such manner as the governor may direct, of the time and place when and where those claiming to be exempt from the performance of military service shall meet the commissioner, and make known the causes and reasons for his exemption, and furnish evidence of their truth. If the commissioner, on investigation, shall be satisfied that the applicant is exempt, he shall note the fact and the cause opposite the name of the applicant upon the roll.

**Those now in service to be deducted.**
The commissioner or commissioners for the county shall write upon the roll opposite the name of any person who, at the time the roll is made, is then in the service of the United States, as an officer or soldier, the words "now in service;" and opposite the name of any person who is then a volunteer for three years' service, and has been enrolled as such upon the rolls of the proper recruiting officer, but has not yet been mustered into the service of the United States as officer or soldier, the words "enrolled as volunteer;" and opposite the name of any person who has served nine months or more in the army of the United States as officer or soldier, and who has resigned or been honorably discharged, the words "has served nine months." In ascertaining the number of persons subject to draft in the county, all those opposite whose names any one of the aforesaid expressions is written, shall be deducted from the whole number on the roll of the county, as well as those exempted from the performance of military service, and the remainder shall constitute the persons subject to draft in the county.

**Commis'r may obtain service of physician, and administer oath.**
The commissioner shall have power to call to his aid a competent practicing physician, and to administer an oath to the party, or to any witness he may produce to testify in his behalf, and if, on any such investigation, the party or witness shall, after being sworn, knowingly and willfully state as a fact any matter or thing which may be relevant, and which is untrue and false, with a view to influence the judgment of the commissioner, and to procure the exemption of the party from the draft, he or she so offending shall be guilty of perjury, and on conviction thereof shall suffer the pains and penalties prescribed by law for that crime.

If there be a number left when the whole number of the company is divided by the number of a class, the number so left shall be denominated supernumeraries, and these supernumeraries shall be taken out of the company by lot before the draft takes place. **Supernumeraries** Those who are supernumeraries, and those who shall constitute a class, shall be determined by lot in the following manner: There shall be as many tickets formed of white paper, of equal size, as there are members in the company. SEC. V. That all persons under twenty-one years of age, convicted of vagrancy, shall be bound out as an apprentice to a master, until the age of twenty-one years, to learn some trade or business, upon the terms which may be agreed upon. The county court clerk shall prepare and keep the indenture, which, when signed, shall have the same effect as indentures of apprenticeship in other cases, and for which the clerk shall have the same fees. SEC. VI. That all persons having the custody and control of vagrants convicted under the provisions of this act shall have the same rights and powers, and be under the same responsibilities, as masters of servants and of apprentices during the time for which they have been hired or bound to them. 168 CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. [TITLE

18. *Embezzlement by agent of corporation*.—The agent of a corporation who fraudulently converts money to his own use "which has been placed under his care and management as such officer," is, under chapter twenty-eight, article twelve, section one, Revised Statutes, guilty of felony, no matter for what purpose it was placed under his care. *Ib.*

49. *Offense defined—agent of private person*.—As to the agent of an individual or private banker the rule is different. Under the second section of the above article the offense defined and punished, consists in fraudulently converting to his own use, money, etc., "before delivery thereof at the place or to the person to whom the same was to be delivered." Under this section the money must have been intrusted to the agent *for delivery* at some place or to some person, in order to constitute the conversion thereof a felony. *Ib.*

20. *Clerk of private banker*.—The clerk of a private banker converted to his own use money which he charged to a depositor on the books of the bank: *Held*—That the offense was not a felony. *Ib.*

### VIII. Gaming.

21. *Gaming with negroes—two degrees of offense*.—An offense of two degrees is defined by the first and second sections of the act of 1858, against gaming with negroes. *Commonwealth v. Garland, 3 Met. 478.*

22. *Offense defined*.—To constitute the higher offense it is necessary that "money or other thing of value," shall have been bet, won, or lost, irrespective of the amount of money or value of the property, and it is not necessary to prove the value as laid. To convict of the lower offense it is only necessary to prove the gaming. *Ib.*

23. *Under indictment for higher, conviction may be for lower degree*.—Under an indictment charging the higher offense under the above statute, the defendant may be convicted of the lower offense. *Ib.*

### IX. Bribery.

24. *Meaning of the term "bribe."*—The term "bribe," as used in the election laws, has a legal signification, and is defined in section eleven, article twelve, of said law. *Commonwealth v. Stephenson, 3 Met. 226.*

25. *Sufficiency of indictment*.—That an election was held at the time and place charged in the indictment appears sufficiently from the averment that the person bribed, did, for said bribe, *vote* for the several persons named in the indictment, and for the several offices. *Ib.*

### X. Peddling without license.

*Indictment for, must state the particular acts*.—The statutes which define who are peddlers, also by necessary implication, specify what acts constitute peddling, and an indictment for peddling without a license must state the particular act or acts intended to be relied on, or it will be insufficient. *Commonwealth v. Dudley, 3 Met. 221.*

172 CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. [TITLE

## XV. Accessories.

57. *Slave may be indicted as accessory.*—A slave may be indicted and tried as an accessory before the fact to the crime of administering poison with intent to kill, and the accessory may be indicted with the principal. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

58. *Punishment of slave accessory.*—In such a case the punishment of the accessory only extends to the infliction of stripes, not exceeding one hundred. *Ib.*

## XVI. Offenses by slaves.

59. *What statutes apply to crimes by slaves.*—None of the provisions of the chapter on crimes and punishments of the Revised Statutes, not in terms applicable to slaves, apply to their punishment, nor change any rule of law in relation to their punishment. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

60. *Larceny by slave a misdemeanor.*—Larceny committed by a slave amounts to a misdemeanor only. *Munford v. Taylor*, 2 Met. 599.

61. *Slaves as accessories to crime.*—A slave may be indicted and tried as an accessory before the fact to the crime of administering poison with intent to kill, committed by another slave, and both may be united in the same indictment. *Commonwealth v. Anthony*, 2 Met. 399.

62. *Punishment of accessory.*—The punishment of such accessory only extends to the infliction of stripes, not exceeding one hundred, as prescribed in chapter ninety-three, Revised Statutes, article seven, section six. *Ib.*

## XVII. Conviction and sentence—construction and effect of.

63. *Two terms of imprisonment—both run at same time.*—Before the adoption of the Code there was no law in force in this state authorizing the courts to adjudge that a term of imprisonment should commence at a future period, or after the expiration of a previous term of confinement. *James v. Ward*, 2 Met. 271.

64. *The same—five years on each indictment means five years only.*—Where, by a verdict on two indictments, a prisoner was found guilty of separate felonies and his punishment assessed by the jury at five years' imprisonment on each, upon which judgment was rendered that "it is considered by the court that the prisoner be confined in the jail and penitentiary house of this commonwealth to hard labor *for the space of five years upon each indictment*:" *Held*—that the legal effect of the judgment was that the prisoner was to be confined five years only; and that both terms of confinement began and terminated at the same time. *Ib.*

65. *Degrees of offense.*—A person indicted for an offense of different degrees, may be convicted of any degree not higher than that charged in the indictment; and of any offense included in the offense charged. *Commonwealth v. Garland*, 3 Met. 478.

472 SHERIFFS. [TITLE

after paying those which had been previously allowed. But when a claim is allowed at the time the levy is imposed, it is not necessary to aver in the notice that the sheriff has collected enough of the county levy to pay it and the other claims allowed. *Thompson v. Healy*, 4 Met. 257.

15. *When sheriff prima facie liable.*—When the county levy has been imposed, and a list of the persons chargeable therewith and of the county debtors and creditors, has been delivered to the sheriff, he is, after the first day of the next October, *prima facie* liable to those whose claims were ordered to be paid at the time the levy was imposed. *Ib.*

16. *Proof on trial of motion.*—On the trial of a motion by county creditor against the sheriff and his sureties, the record containing the list of claims may be read in evidence, and it may be proved by the clerk that he delivered a copy thereof to the sheriff without producing the copy. *Ib.*

17. *What notice must aver.*—The plaintiff must aver in his notice, and prove every fact necessary to show the sheriff liable. He must aver that the list of the persons liable to the payment of the levy, the sum to be paid by each, and a list of the sums due, and from whom due to the county, have been delivered by the clerk to the sheriff. *Ib.*

18. *Demand on deputy.*—In such cases a demand upon the sheriff is necessary, and a demand upon his deputy is not sufficient. *Ib.*

19. *Where no demand can be made.*—If a demand can not be made upon the sheriff, the creditor can recover from his sureties the debt without damages in an ordinary action, upon showing that the sheriff did, or might have collected a sufficient sum to pay the county creditors. *Argu.* *Ib.*

XXXIX.] CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS. 173

**XVIII. Appeals. Bills of exceptions. Immaterial errors.**

66. *What errors will reverse.*—A judgment will not be reversed because of an error in rejecting evidence merely pertinent or relevant, or technically admissible. It must be *important* to the party seeking the reversal, in view of the whole case. *Champ v. The Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 17.

67. *Exceptions—when to be taken.*—To authorize a reversal of a judgment convicting of felony because of an error in admitting important testimony, the record must show that exceptions were taken thereto at the time. *Clem v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 10.

68. *What bill of exceptions must show.*—The court of appeals can not disturb a judgment of conviction for errors in instructing and refusing to instruct the jury, unless it appears from the record that it furnishes every instruction given to the jury. *Ib.*

69. *The same.*—Where the bill of exceptions states that "the court gave the following instructions,"—going on to enumerate them—"to each and all of which the defendant objected, but the court overruled the objection and the defendant at the time excepted and still excepts:" *Held*—That the bill does not in terms, or by fair deduction, show that it contains *all* the instructions. *Ib.*

70. *Exceptions—when to be taken.*—Unless the bill of exceptions show that the defendant objected to instructions moved by the commonwealth and excepted to the ruling of the court overruling his objection at the time, he will be considered as having waived any error committed by the court to his prejudice. *Burns v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 13.

**XIX. Escape. Surrender of prisoner into custody.**

71. *Surrender of prisoner by bail—what amounts to.*—A defendant appeared upon the day named in the recognizance and pleaded to the indictment, was put upon trial and by order of court was placed in the custody of the sheriff during the trial. After the jury had retired he escaped from the sheriff and failed to appear for judgment upon the verdict found: *Held*—That the sureties in his recognizance were not liable. *Commonwealth v. Coleman*, 2 Met. 382.

72. *The same.*—But the mere appearance of the defendant in discharge of his recognizance, until the court orders him into custody, does not discharge the sureties. *Ib.* 382.

See **APPEALS**, notes 10, 11, 12, 13. **CONSTABLE**, notes 2, 3. **CONSTITUTIONAL LAW**, note 25. **GAMING**, notes. **EXAMINING COURT**, notes 1, 2, 3, 4.

VII.** It shall be the duty of the auditor to make out a report at the end of every six months, showing the names of claimants, with the aggregate amount of claims filed in his office, which report he shall hand to the governor, whose duty it shall be to lay the same before the legislature, from time to time, recommending such action thereon as he may deem necessary.

VIII.** No claim shall be so filed with the clerk of the county court until the claimant has procured the written certificate on said claim from some county or circuit clerk, or judge, to the effect that he knows said claimant personally, and believes his claim to be just, and that he is a loyal person, and that such clerk or judge is not, in any way, interested in said claim.

**Object of this act**
**SEC. IX.** The intention of this act is not, at present, to assume the payment of all the claims due to all the loyal citizens of Kentucky, embraced in the above provisions; but it is to legalize a mode of ascertaining the amount of such claims in the state, and to whom due, and to legalize a mode of proving the same without any cost to the state, so that any future legislature may be able to act advisedly should the state choose hereafter to consider the question of assuming to pay these claims, and look to the general government for reimbursement.

**SEC. X.** This act shall be in force from its passage.

AN ACT to amend the law regulating the taking of depositions out of this state.

That where depositions have been taken out of this state to be used in this commonwealth, any such deposition or depositions may, after being sealed up by the officer taking the same, and directed to the clerk, or court, or justice, where or before whom the action is pending, in which the deposition is to be used, be delivered to the party taking the same, his agent or attorney; and when deposited by such party, his agent or attorney, with such clerk, court, or justice, shall be sufficient to authorize the reading of such deposition or depositions upon the trial, if, in other respects, they have been legally taken: *Provided*, That the person or persons conveying and delivering the depositions shall take an oath that the same have not been opened by him, nor by any other person, to his knowledge or belief.

AN ACT providing for the investment of certain funds belonging to the state, with a view to increasing the resources of the sinking fund. Preamble.

WHEREAS, a large sum in coin will shortly be realized to the commonwealth, from the stock which it owns in the Southern Bank of Kentucky, the profits on which stock formed a large part of the resources of the sinking fund, and to prevent the diminishing of that fund—

SEC. I. That the commissioners of the sinking fund may, as soon as the same, or any part thereof, shall be realized, proceed to sell said coin, on the best terms to be had in the money market, for United States currency, and then to pay the banks of this state any portion of the military loan due said banks by the state, and to invest the balance in Kentucky state bonds, the bonds of the United States, known as "5-20 bonds," or other government securities; and that the interest in these bonds, when paid, shall become a part of the sinking fund of this commonwealth. Commissioners of sinking fund may sell coin belonging to state.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage.

1860.
*March 1.*

**1** AN ACT to amend the criminal code of practice.

Appellate jurisdiction of court of appeals extended.

SEC. I. That the court of appeals shall have appellate jurisdiction in all prosecutions for misdemeanors, except where the only punishment is either a fine of fifty dollars, or not exceeding that amount, or imprisonment not exceeding thirty days, or both fine and imprisonment not exceeding the amounts above specified.
SEC. II. This act shall take effect from its passage.

1861.
*December 16.*

*[Court of appeals may extend time of filing record beyond second term.]*

**2** AN ACT to amend section eight hundred and seventy-nine of the civil code of practice.

Stanton's code of Prac. 294.
Law forbidding court of appeals extending time for filing records beyond first day of second term repealed.

SEC. I. That so much of section eight hundred and seventy-nine of the civil code of practice, which forbids the court of appeals from extending to appellants time for filing transcripts of records beyond the first day of the second term of said court after the appeal is granted, be and the same is hereby repealed.
SEC. II. That this act shall apply to all civil cases in said court, whether the appeal has been granted before or since the date hereof; and the same shall take effect from its passage.

That in all actions, suits, or proceedings (including proceedings to perpetuate evidence), now pending, or which may be hereafter instituted, in any of the courts within this commonwealth, in which depositions may be read in evidence, and such depositions have been filed in the proper office, as is now directed by law, and any such deposition being mislaid, lost, or stolen, so that the same can not be used, a copy of said deposition, certificate, and indorsements thereon, duly certified as such by the clerk or officer having custody of the original deposition, together with the seal of office, may be used and read in all respects as though it were the original.

SEC. III. The governor, for the money which he may borrow under the provisions of this act, shall issue and deliver to the lender the bond or bonds of the state, payable to him or bearer, bearing interest at the rate of six per centum per annum, payable semi-annually, on the first Mondays in January and July, at the public treasury of the state, or at such other place as the governor may contract. APPEAL.
[TITLE

I. APPEALS TO INFERIOR COURTS.

II. APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS.

I. *To Inferior Courts.*

1. *Where court appealed from had no jurisdiction*—If the quarterly court has no jurisdiction of the case, no recovery can be had upon an appeal to the circuit court although the circuit court would have had jurisdiction of the case had the action been brought in it in the first instance. In such a case the circuit court should make such orders as will render the proceedings in the quarterly court inoperative. *Wigginton v. Moss*, 2 Met. 38.

2. *Same*—*what order should be made on the appeal*.—Upon an appeal from the judgment of a justice of the peace, if it appear that the justice had no jurisdiction, the court should dismiss the suit. *Fleming v. Limebaugh*, 2 Met. 265.

3. *Amount in controversy*.—The amount in controversy was over $50; and the magistrate dismissed the warrant; the plaintiff appealed to the quarterly court, which likewise dismissed him, and he appealed to the circuit court which gave him judgment. The action of the justice and quarterly court was correct, and the circuit court should have dismissed the case for want of jurisdiction. *Fidler v. Hall*, 2 Met. 461.

4. *Upon trial of appeal, cause of action can not be changed*.—Upon the trial of an appeal from a justice of the peace, the cause of action must be the same as that set out in the warrant. The same action is to be tried anew; but no new action can be tried, neither can the demand be increased by pleading or otherwise beyond the jurisdiction of the justice. *Burbage v. Squires*, 3 Met. 77.

5. *Appeal from writ of ad quod damnum*.—Sections 30 and 31 of chapter 103 *Revised Statutes*, allowing either party to traverse an inquest on a writ of *ad quod damnum*, and an appeal from the decision on the traverse, are not repealed by the civil code. *Robb v. Turnpike Co.*, 3 Met. 117.

6. *Cost on appeal*.—On the trial of an appeal from the judgment of a justice, if the judgment is more favorable to the appellant than the one appealed from, he is entitled to his cost. *Kellar v. Bate*, 3 Met. 130.

7. *Appeals in will cases*.—On an appeal from the order of a county court admitting an instrument to record as a will, it is unnecessary to file a petition. A transcript of the proceedings of the county court, with the names of the appellants and appellees must be filed with the clerk of the circuit court, and process served on the appellees. *Jones v. Jones*, 3 Met. 266.

8. *In road cases*.—An appeal lies to the circuit court from an order of the county court allowing a gate to be erected across a public road. *Bond v. Mullins*, 3 Met. 282.

VII.] APPEAL. 31 ## II. To the Court of Appeals. 9. *When supersedeas to issue.*—The clerk of the court of appeals has no authority to issue a supersedeas until a copy of the record has been filed in his office. *Hunt v. Berryman*, 2 Met. 239. 10. *An attorney may appeal from order striking his name from rolls.*—An appeal lies to the court of appeals from an order of a circuit court striking the name of an attorney at law from the rolls of such court upon charges of malpractice. *Turner v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 619. 11. *In misdemeanors, record must be brought up in sixty days.*—Neither the commonwealth nor the defendant can maintain an appeal from a judgment on a prosecution for a misdemeanor, unless the record is filed in the clerk's office of the court of appeals within sixty days after the rendition of the judgment. *Commonwealth v. McCready*, 2 Met. 376. 12. *Rule supra can not be changed by agreement of counsel.*—In the case *supra*, the fact that the counsel on both sides agreed to the entry of an order on the record allowing time until the next term of the court to prepare and file the bill of exceptions, does not change the rule. *Ib.* 13. *Immaterial errors.*—A party whose substantial rights have not been prejudiced by a judgment, can not maintain an appeal therefrom. *Combs v. Draining Co.*, 3 Met. 72. 14. *Order suspending jailer may be appealed from.*—A jailer, who has been permanently suspended in the exercise of his office by an order of the county court, may maintain an appeal from such order. *Low v. Commonwealth*, 3 Met. 237. 15. *Appeals from judgment of Jefferson county court.*—Where the matter in controversy is over $20, an appeal lies to the court of appeals from a judgment of the Jefferson county court. *Gross v. Jones*, 3 Met. 295. 16. *When court of appeals has no jurisdiction.*—Where the judgment appealed from is less than $50, and was not reduced below that sum by counter-claim or setoff, the court of appeals has no jurisdiction. *Revell v. Pettit*, 3 Met. 314. 17. *What is a final order.*—An order dissolving an injunction before the disposition of the case in which it was granted, is not final and can not be appealed from. The party should apply for its reinstatement as authorized by the code, sections 326, 327. *Rodman v. Forline*, 2 Met. 325. 18. *The same.*—Where an order overruling a motion for a new trial, does not finally dispose of the case, no appeal can be maintained from it. *Fernold v. Speer*, 3 Met. 459. 19. *The same.*—A judgment giving priority to one creditor over another out of an attached fund, but which does not distribute the fund nor give other relief to either party, is not final. *Hanson v. Bowman*, 4 Met. 108. See CRIMES, PENALTIES, ETC., Notes 66, 67, 68, 69, 70. SEC. I. That it shall be the duty of the officers or managers of the banking associations which have or may hereafter be established within this state, under the act of congress, entitled "an act to provide a national currency secured by a pledge of United States bonds, and to provide for the circulation and redemption thereof," to file with the auditor of public accounts a statement, verified by the affidavit of the presiding officer or cashier of the association, showing the amount of its capital stock, and the number of shares into which the same may be divided; and it shall be the duty of such officers or managers, annually, on the first Monday in July, to pay into the public treasury of the state, in aid of the sinking fund, a tax of fifty cents on each share thereof, equal to one hundred dollars of the capital stock of said association: *Provided however*, That if the capital stock shall at any time be increased or diminished, it shall be the duty of such association to revise and amend the statement required by this act, so as to exhibit at all times the true amount of capital stock held by such association.

SEC. II. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

SEC. I. That hereafter no slave shall be deemed to be emancipated by the laws of this state, until the person emancipating such slave, or some person for him, as principal, with good and sufficient resident security, before the county court of the county of his residence, shall give a covenant to the commonwealth, covenanting that such person shall remove such slave (naming him or her, and giving the age, color, height, and weight) beyond the limits of this state within ninety days after the approval of such covenant by said county court; nor until such slave shall subscribe a written statement, to be indorsed on such covenant, that he or she does then forever abandon his or her residence in, and will, within the time named, remove from this state, which subscription shall be attested by the clerk of such court; upon the breach of such covenant, it shall be the duty of the attorney for the commonwealth of the district, and the attorney for such county, or either of them, to institute suit upon the same for the recovery of such damages as may be found to be due: *Provided*, That the amount of damages shall not be less than the value of such slave at the time of his or her emancipation, which damages shall be paid into the county treasury, after deducting therefrom twenty per cent. as a compensation for the attorney or attorneys by whom the suit is prosecuted, and also ten dollars to be paid to the person or persons who may have given information of the breach of the covenant.

wife by the husband, is, in the absence of any expression in the will or necessary inference to the contrary, presumed to have been intended as in part of her dowable or distributable share of the estate. *Ib.*

3. *What is a jointure.*—Any conveyance or devise of real or personal estate intended by the grantor to be in lieu of dower must be deemed a conveyance or devise by way of jointure within the meaning of the statute. *Tevis v. McCreary, 3 Met. 151.*

4. *Question of intention.*—Whether a gift was made in satisfaction of dower is a question of intention to be gathered in general from the face of the instrument passing the title. It is not necessary that the instrument should state in terms that the gift is in lieu of dower. *Ib.*

5. *Wife deprived of jointure.*—If the wife be lawfully deprived of her jointure or any part of it, without any fault of her own, she may have indemnity by way of dower or damage out of her husband's estate, to the extent of the loss. *Ib.*

6. *Legacy as jointure entitled to priority.*—Where a legacy was given to the wife by way of jointure and the assets are insufficient to pay all the legacies, the legacy given as a jointure will be entitled to priority over the general legacies. *Ib.*

## II. Widow's right before dower assigned.

7. *Rent—from what time recoverable.*—A widow, whose husband died since the Revised Statutes took effect, is entitled to rent against a purchaser from her husband, from the time she commences her action to recover dower (Revised Statutes, 394). *Yancey v. Smith, 2 Met. 408.*

## III. Dower in incumbered property—assigning by executors.

8. *If wife mortgage dower, entitled to be reimbursed out of surplus.*—Though the wife join with her husband in a mortgage of his land, and though the husband should afterward release, convey, or devise the equity of redemption, the wife will be entitled at his decease to her dower in the land, subject to the mortgage; and if the land be sold under the mortgage, then to her claim as for dower in any surplus proceeds. *Harrow v. Johnson, 3 Met. 578.*

9. *Wife joining husband in mortgage—right to have personalty first exhausted.*—Where the wife joins with her husband in a mortgage of his land and personalty, she has, after the death of her husband, a right to have the mortgaged personalty first applied to the mortgaged debt, as against the mortgagee and general creditors. *Ib.*

10. *Executors may convey to wife in satisfaction of dower.*—Where the title of the testator's lands are vested in the executors with power to sell, they can assign a part thereof to the widow for dower, in consideration of her releasing the residue, which in the absence of collusion between them and the widow, will bind the heirs and creditors. *Ib.*

8 AN ACT to prevent certain negroes and mulattoes from migrating to or remaining in this state.

Negroes or mulattoes claiming freedom under the President's proclamation to be treated and dealt with as runaway slaves.
SEC. I. That it shall not be lawful for any negro or mulatto, claiming or pretending to be free, under or by virtue of the proclamation of the president of the United States, dated the 1st of January, 1863, declaring free slaves in certain states and parts of states, or any similar proclamation, by order of the government of the United States, or any officer or agent thereof, to migrate to or remain in this state.

SEC. II. Any negro or mulatto who shall violate the provisions of this act shall be arrested, dealt with, and disposed of as runaways, and the proceedings shall conform to the laws in existence, at the time they are had in relation to runaway slaves.

Purchaser's title declared good.
SEC. III. The purchaser of any such negro or mulatto sold under and by virtue of such proceedings shall, by virtue of such purchase, acquire and have the same right to, the same property in, and control over such negro or mulatto as masters have over their slaves under existing laws, subject to the provisions in relation to slaves sold as runaways, and shall in all respects be governed by the law in relation to master and slave.

When purchase money to be paid
SEC. IV. The purchase money for such negro or mulatto shall not be paid into the public treasury until the right of redemption shall have expired or been finally determined by adjudication, in case the same shall be put in litigation, as provided in relation to slaves sold as runaways, and the court shall have power to loan the same, in the meantime taking bond and good security for the same.

SEC. V. It shall be the duty of all peace officers to see that the provisions of this act are enforced.

SEC. VI. This act shall take effect from its passage.

AN ACT to amend an act entitled "An act to amend the Code of practice **5** in civil cases," approved December 23, 1861.

Simms*, 4 Met. 285. 4. *Four months' absence from home—not out of the state.*—Where the debtor leaves his home with the intention of going out of the state, and does go out of the state, and is absent *from his home* four months pursuant to such intention, it must be regarded as absence from the state, and ground of attachment, although he may have been delayed a few days by some unlooked-for casualty in passing the territorial limits of the state, so that he had not been actually out of the state four months when the attachment issued. *Ib.* ## II. Sufficiency of the affidavit—Amendment thereof. 5. *That the affiant "believes" the facts, etc.*—The affidavit stated that "the affiant *believed* that defendant R. has left C. county, the county of his residence, to avoid the service of legal process. He believes that R. so conceals himself that a summons can not be served on him," which statement was sworn to as true, and the defendant responded to it without objection to its sufficiency, and the evidence showed that the statement was true: *Held*—that the judgment of the circuit court sustaining the attachment must be affirmed. *Ryan v. Bean*, 2 Met. 137. 6. *Affidavit by agent—what must be stated.*—Where an affidavit to obtain an attachment is made by an agent of the plaintiff, it must state that the plaintiff is then absent from the county in which the action is brought, and that the affiant is his agent. *Pool v. Webster*, 3 Met. 278. 7. *Amendment of affidavit.*—Where the affidavit upon which the attachment issued is defective, it can not be amended so as to render it valid after the attachment issues. *Ib.* [But see post, and *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342.] 8. *Affidavit may be amended.*—The affidavit must state that the claim is just. To omit this statement is cause for reversal. But upon the return of the cause to the lower court, the plaintiff may amend the affidavit (*Civil Code, section 161*). *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342. 9. *The same.*—There is no difference in principle between allowing an amendment of the petition on which an attachment issues, and the amendment of an affidavit on which an attachment issues. *Ib.*; and *Civil Code, section 161*. 10. *Defective affidavit—attachment not void.*—An attachment is not void which issues upon a defective affidavit. [TITLE ### III. *Levy*. 11. *Officer presumed to have done his duty*.—The officer stated in his return that he had levied the attachment on a town lot, "and posted a copy of the attachment on the premises, there being no tenant," but omitted to state that he left the copy in a conspicuous place on the premises. *Held*, that, nothing appearing to the contrary, the law presumed the officer to have performed his duty, and that the return was sufficient. *Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met. 284. ### IV. *Forthcoming bond and bond for the retention of attached property*. 12. *Bond discharges attachment, and admits the grounds on which it issued*.—The execution of the bond for the retention of attached property as authorized by sections two hundred and forty-two and two hundred and forty-three of the Code, operates to discharge the attachment, and the obligors in the bond must perform the judgment of the court. In an action on the bond they can neither contest the grounds for obtaining the attachment, nor the liability of the property to be taken under it. *Hazelrigg v. Donaldson*, 2 Met. 445. 13. *Forthcoming bond—effect of*.—Possession of property taken under a general attachment may be retained upon executing the bond provided by section two hundred and thirty-five, which, in effect, binds the obligors to have the property forthcoming to answer the judgment of the court; or that the obligors will pay the damage resulting from the default. *Bell v. The Wrecking Co.*, 3 Met. 558. 14. *Forthcoming bond and bond discharging attachment—distinction*.—The bond *supra* is a mere obligation for the forthcoming of the property; but if the defendant desires to obtain *a discharge of the attachment*, he may do so by giving bond "to the effect that he will *perform the judgment of the court*." (*Code, sections* 242, 243, 254.) This bond stands in lieu of the attachment, and the action proceeds as though no attachment had ever issued. *Ib*. 15. *Forthcoming bond does not discharge attachment*.—In attachments against steamboats, the bond authorized by section two hundred and sixty-eight is merely an obligation for the forthcoming of the property, and does not discharge the attachment, while the bond authorized by sections two hundred and seventy and two hundred and seventy-one discharges the attachment, and is an undertaking to perform the judgment of the court. *Ib*. 16. *Construction of informal bond*.—In an action brought to foreclose a chattel mortgage, a specific attachment issued and was levied on the property as authorized by section two hundred and seventy-three. Simms*, 2 Met. 209. 18. *Mistake*—*wrong bond executed*—*party bound*.—A party whose property was seized under an attachment against another, offered to execute the forthcoming bond as allowed by *Code*, section two hundred and thirty-five, which the sheriff refused to accept, or to surrender the property, unless the bond prescribed in sections two hundred and forty-two and two hundred and forty-three, was executed, which bond he was thus coerced to execute and did execute as surety of the defendant in the attachment, not understanding its legal effect at the time: *Held*—that notwithstanding the coercion and the ignorance of the nature and effect of the bond, he was bound unconditionally to perform the judgment of the court. *Hazelrigg v. Donaldson*, 2 Met. 445. ### V. *Claim of attached property by stranger.* 19. *Irregularity of proceedings*—*effect*.—A. attached the property of B. A third party claimed the property, filed his petition to be made a defendant to the suit and was ordered by the court to be made a party, *but never was in fact made such*, though he was regarded by the plaintiff (who raises the question) and the court, as a party; and the action was tried as though he was such: *Held*—that the irregularity was no ground for reversal. *Schwein v. Simms*, 2 Met. 209. 20. *Jury*—*effect of verdict*.—Where a third party claims attached property, the court may order a jury to try the fact, and a verdict in favor of the claimant must be regarded as finding that the claimant was the owner and entitled to the possession of the property when the attachment was levied, although the verdict does not specify that he was the owner at that time. *Ib.* ### VI. *Trial of attachment. Practice.* 21. *Evidence on trial of attachment*.—Affidavits can not be used as evidence on the trial of attachments. The proof must be by deposition or by the oral examination of the witnesses in court. *Newton v. ATTACHMENT.
[TITLE

be developed and shown by further pleading and proof. *Roberts v. Drinkard*, 3 Met. 309.

23. *Affidavits constitute pleadings*—*no other effect*.—On the trial of an attachment the affidavits of plaintiff and defendant constitute the pleadings and have no other effect. They can not be used as evidence. *Duncan v. Wickliff*, 4 Met. 118.

24. *Failure to contest attachment*—*effect*.—If the defendant in an attachment appears and fails to contest it, the court must regard it as valid. *Spalding v. Simms*, 4 Met. 285.

25. *Must be warning order before attachment sustained*.—An order sustaining an attachment against a non-resident who has not been summoned or appeared, is erroneous unless there has been a warning order against him. *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met. 342.

## VII. Liens and priorities.

26. *Sale in contemplation of insolvency*—*no priority obtained by attachment*.—A sale made in contemplation of insolvency operates under the statute as an assignment by the vendor for the equal benefit of all his creditors, and neither of them can thereafter obtain priority over their co-creditors by suing out attachments. *Shouse v. Utterback*, 2 Met. 52.

27. *The same*.—The rule is the same although the attachments were sued out and levied before any action had been brought under the statute to have the sale declared an assignment, provided such action is brought at any time within the six months prescribed by the statute. *Ib*.

28. *Attachment of debt assigned by parol*.—Attaching creditors acquire only an equitable right to a debt in the hands of the garnishee; and this equity is subordinate to that of a surety of the defendant to whom the defendant had made a parol assignment of the debt before the suing out of the attachment, as an indemnity for his suretyship. *Newby & Co. v. Hill & Co.*, 2 Met. 530.

29. *Attachment of steamboat*—*lien for supplies*.—An attachment to enforce a lien upon a steamboat for supplies is subordinate to the lien of a prior mortgagor, and where a forthcoming bond has been given and the boat is not produced, the value of the equity of redemption should be ascertained by reference to a master. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

## VIII. Sale of attached property.

Powell*, 3 Met. 124.

## IX. *Damage for wrongful attachment.*

31. *Cost and counsel fee recoverable.*—The only damage to which a defendant was subjected by the wrongful issue and levy of an attachment upon his land, was the employment of counsel to defend the attachment. In an action against the surety in the bond: *Held*—that he is responsible for the costs, and reasonable counsel fee in defending the attachment. *Trapnall v. McAffee*, 3 Met. 34.

32. *What damage recoverable.*—The only damage which can be recovered in such an action, is what results from the attachment itself; not what is occasioned by the action independent of the attachment. *Ib.*

33. *Only reasonable attorney's fee recoverable.*—The defendant in an attachment wrongfully sued out, can only recover his attorney's fees where he has paid, or contracted to pay them, and upon showing that the amount is reasonable. *Shultz v. Morrison*, 3 Met. 98.

34. *When action for damage may be brought.*—An action on an attachment bond for maliciously suing out an attachment, will not lie until the attachment has been discharged, and the plaintiff in such action must aver that it has been discharged. *Nolle v. Thompson*, 3 Met. 121.

35. *Counter-claim for damage—not sustained in attachment suit.*—Defendant in an action in which an attachment issued against his property, can not set up as a counter-claim in that action, a demand for damages for maliciously suing out the attachment, although the plaintiff is a non-resident and has no property in the state out of which the damage can be made. *Ibid.*

## X. *Attachments against steamboats.*

36. *Forthcoming bond does not preclude from contesting attachment.*—The claimant of a steamboat taken by attachment, by executing the bond prescribed by the Code, section two hundred and sixty-eight, is not precluded from asserting his right to the boat, or contesting the validity of the attachment. *Halbert v. McCulloch*, 3 Met. 456.

37. *Informal bond construed.*—The obligors in the bond prescribed by section two hundred and sixty-eight of the Code, undertaking that they will pay the plaintiffs such sum of money as may be adjudged in the action or have the boat forthcoming for the satisfaction of such judgment, whichever the court may order, are only liable for the forthcoming of the boat for the purpose of subjecting the defendant's interest therein to the satisfaction of the attachment lien. *Ib.*

## XI. *Attachments after a return of "no property."*

38. *Lien created by levy.*—The levy of an attachment sued out after the return of an execution "No property found," creates a lien upon the property of the debtor levied on, which the plaintiff can enforce against all subsequent purchasers. *Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met. 284.

I. BY ONE OF TWO JOINT OWNERS OF A SLAVE. EFFECT OF.
II. EMANCIPATION UPON A CONDITION. PERFORMANCE OF CONDITION.
III. CHILDREN BORN BEFORE EMANCIPATION TAKES EFFECT.
IV. DEVISE OF A SLAVE TO A FREE NEGRO.

---

### I. By one of two joint owners of a slave. Effect of.

1. *Amounts to a conversion, and slave free.*—A person owning a three-fifths' interest in certain slaves devised their freedom to them: *Held*—that the slaves were thereby emancipated; that the act of the testator was a conversion of his co-tenants' interests to his own use, and changed their interest in the slaves into a money demand on his estate. *Oatfield v. Waring*, 14 Johnson's R. 188; *Davis v. Tingle*, 8 B. Mon. 544; *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 593.

### II. Emancipation upon a condition. Performance of the condition.

2. *Condition must be faithfully performed.*—A testator, in 1837, declared in his will, that any of his slaves at certain periods and ages who wished to be free, "should be delivered to the colonization society to be transported to Liberia. None of them are to be forced to go. Those of them who do not choose to go to Liberia, are to continue to serve, etc., until they are willing and do go:" *Held*—that to entitle the slaves to freedom, they must go to Liberia, in good faith to dwell there and become colonists; that to go with a design to return immediately to the United States, which design is immediately carried out, is not a performance of the condition upon which the slave was to be free. *Winn v. Sam Martin*, 4 Met. 231.

---

3. *Remaining in free state—effect.*—That the negro, on his return from Liberia, stopped some three months in New York before returning to Kentucky, of which his owner had knowledge, but made no effort to regain his possession, did not give the slave his right to freedom. *Ib.*

### III. *Children born before emancipation of mother takes effect.*

4. *Child born of slave mother is a slave.*—A will contained this clause: "It is further my wish and desire that negro *Diner* shall serve six years after the day of my death," when she was to be free: *Held*—that a child born of Diner during the six years after testator's death, was a slave. *Jane a (slave) v. Prather, 2 Met. 453.*

5. *The same.*—It is well settled in this and other states where slavery exists, that the child takes the condition of the mother at the time of its birth. *Ib.*

### IV. *Devise of slave to free negro.*

6. *Free negro can not take a slave by devise.*—A devise of a slave to a free negro is void. Such a devise confers no right of freedom to the slave. *Jane v. Prather, 2 Met. 453.*

502 SLAVES, RUNAWAYS AND FREE NEGROES. [TITLE

2. *Reward for arrest of runaway*.—To entitle a person arresting a runaway in a border county, who has escaped from another county, to the reward prescribed by the act of 1856, he must deliver the slave to the owner, or person from whom he escaped, or into the jail of the county where the owner resides. *Nall v. Proctor*, 3 Met. 447.

3. *Delivery of runaway*—*reward*.—A party is not entitled to the reward provided in the above act who arrests the runaway and delivers him into the jail of the county where the arrest was made, although the jailer deliver him afterward to the owner. *Ib*.

4. *Owner's liability for medical services to slaves*.—It is only in cases requiring instant and indispensable assistance, and not admitting delay to obtain the master's assent, that the latter would be liable for medical services rendered his slaves without his consent. *Kellar v. Bate*, 3 Met. 130.

5. *Acquiescence is consent*.—But if a master knows that medical services are being rendered to his slaves, and permits it to be continued without objection, or shows a willingness or desire that the services should be rendered, he is presumed to consent. *Ib*.

6. *Loss of slave*—*criterion of damage*.—In an action by the owner of a life estate in a slave against a constable for negligently suffering the slave to escape while in his custody under a criminal charge whereby the slave is lost, the measure of damage is the value of such life estate. *Tudor v. Lewis*, 3 Met. 378.

7. *Selling dower slaves*.—A doweress who sells a dower slave to a southern trader, who removes the slave out of the state without the consent of the person owning the reversion, does thereby forfeit her life estate in such slave. *Williams v. McClanahan*, 3 Met. 420.

8. *The same*—*reversioner's right*.—In the case *supra*, if the doweress invest the money received upon such sale in other slaves, the owner in reversion has a right to take such slaves instead of the money for which the dower slave was sold. *Ib*.

9. *Suit for division*.—In general a suit for the division of slaves can not be maintained until an administrator is appointed and made party; but cases may arise where this will not apply. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

10. *Emancipation by one joint tenant*—*effect*.—If one of several tenants in common of slaves devise them their freedom, it is a conversion of the slaves to his own use. The slaves will be free, and the co-tenants will be entitled to be paid the value of their interest out of the testator's estate. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

AN ACT to prevent procuring substitutes in this state for persons out of this state.

**Substitute broker**

If any substitute broker, by himself or agent, directly or indirectly, shall, for the purpose of procuring any person as a substitute who may be a resident of the state, or domiciled in the state at the time, procure or attempt to procure any person as a substitute for any person not drafted in this state, or not enrolled for draft in this state, or not resident in this state, such persons, their aiders and abettors, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and shall, upon the conviction of a jury, be fined any sum not exceeding one thousand dollars, or be imprisoned not exceeding one year, or be so fined and imprisoned, at the discretion of the jury.

SEC. III. This act to be in force from its passage.

I. WHO ARE SURETIES. WHEN BOUND. SIGNING UPON CONDITION. II. RELEASE AND DISCHARGE OF SURETIES. III. REMEDY OF SURETY AGAINST PRINCIPAL. ### I. Who are sureties. When bound. Signing on condition. *Where surety signs as principal.*—Where the obligors in an assignable instrument hold themselves out as principals, both the assignor and assignee of the instrument have a right to treat them as such, unless they know that some of the parties are sureties of the others. *Neel v. Harding*, 2 Met. 247. 2. *Signing on condition that others sign.*—Where sureties signed the note on condition that two other solvent persons should also sign it, of which condition and its breach the holder had notice before he took the note: *Held*—that he could not recover against the sureties. *Coffman v. Wilson*, 2 Met. 542; *Bivins v. Helsley*, 4 Met. 78. 3. *The same—notice.*—Where the surety signed upon condition that principal was to get another to sign, and that the note was not to be delivered unless the other did sign, and the principal delivered the note to the obligee without procuring it to be signed by the other, of which condition and violation the obligee had no notice when he took the obligation: *Held*—that the surety was bound. *Millett v. Parker*, 2 Met. 608. 4. *The same—notice.*—But if the obligee when he takes the note has notice that the surety had signed and was to be bound only on condition that another should also sign, which he had not done, the surety will not be bound to him. *Bivins v. Helsley*, 4 Met. 78. ### II. Release and discharge of sureties. 5. *Indulgence where surety signs as principal.*—A contract of indulgence between the assignee of a joint and several note and the principal debtor, without the assent of the surety, does not release the latter unless the assignee had knowledge that he was a surety at the time of granting the indulgence. *Neel v. Harding*, 2 Met. 247. 6. *Release of surety by replevying judgment.*—Where a judgment against principal and surety is replevied by the principal debtor with new sureties, the replevin bond merges the judgment and releases the original surety. *Havens v. Fondry*, 4 Met. 247. 33 # INDEX.

McKay, 2 Met. 294.* 9. *The same—mutual debts—insolvency—set off.*—The insolvency of the principal at the time he assigns a note held by him upon his surety, does not entitle the latter to an equity against the note in the hands of the assignee, he not having paid the debt for which he was surety until after he had notice of the assignment. *Ib.* 10. *Co-sureties—liability inter se.*—A surety who pays his principal's debt, must, in order to compel his co-surety to contribute his proportion of the debt so paid, show that the principal is insolvent. The Revised Statutes (chapter ninety-seven, section seven), have not changed this rule. *Lee v. Foreman, 3 Met. 114.* 11. *Payment by surety after he is released.*—Where a surety, who has by operation of law, been released from all obligation to pay the debt, pays the same, he can not recover it from his principal. *Kimble v. Cummings, 3 Met. 327.* 12. *The same—surety in replevin bond.*—Execution issued upon a replevin bond after twelve months from its maturity and was levied on property of the surety, who thereupon paid the debt: *Held*—in the absence of any assignment of the debt to the surety, or of any request by the principal to him to pay it, or of a subsequent promise of the principal to pay it, that he can not recover against the principal. *Ib.* 13. *Payment by surety entitles him to securities held by creditor.*—When a surety has paid the debt of his principal he is entitled to stand in the place of the creditor as to all liens and equities to which the creditor had a right to look as security for payment of the debt. *Havens v. Fondry, 4 Met. 247.* 14. *The same.*—In such a case it must be shown that at the time the surety paid the debt, the creditor had a valid and subsisting lien or equity enforceable in a court of equity, in order to entitle the surety to the benefits of it. *Ib.* See LIMITATION, Notes 13, 14, 15. BASTARDY.
[TITLE

1. *Bastardy cases not actions but special proceedings.*—A bastardy case is not an action but a special proceeding as defined by the Code. It is not for a penalty or forfeiture or to enforce a private right within the meaning of the Code. *Chandler v. Commonwealth*, 4 Met. 66.

2. *Bastard father liable though under age.*—The infancy of a bastard father does not excuse him from prosecution under the bastardy statutes for the support of his child. Infants are liable for the wrongs they commit. But in such a prosecution a guardian *ad litem* should be appointed to defend for him. *Ib*.

3. *Court may render judgment though bastard father fail to appear.*—Bastardy proceedings under *chapter six of the Revised Statutes*, are civil and not criminal proceedings, and the appearance of the defendant is not necessary to authorize a trial, but the court may proceed to judgment, and enforce its judgment by rule and attachment. *Ib*.

4. *Absence in army does not authorize continuance.*—The absence of a defendant in a bastardy case as a soldier in the armies of the state or United States, does not entitle him to a continuance under the acts of March 8 and 11, 1862. *Ib*.

5. *Evidence—what incompetent.*—A promise of marriage is not admissible evidence on a trial of a bastardy case. *Ib*.

6. *Appeals lie from judgment on forfeited recognizance.*—An appeal from a judgment of the county court on a forfeited recognizance for the appearance of defendant in a bastardy case, lies to the court of appeals and not to the circuit court. *Commonwealth v. Taphorn*, 4 Met. 71.

EXECUTION.
[TITLE

I. TO WHOM TO BE DIRECTED.
II. LEVY AND SALE: VALIDITY OF.
III. SALE OF EQUITABLE INTERESTS UNDER EXECUTION.
IV. INDEMNITY TO OFFICER BEFORE LEVY OR SALE.
V. STAY OF EXECUTION BY INJUNCTION OR SUPERSEDEAS.
VI. PENALTY FOR FAILING TO RETURN.
VII. DEATH OF SHERIFF: COMPLETION OF EXECUTION.

I. *To whom to be directed.*

1. *When execution to be directed to jailer or coroner.*—Where the original process in a case is served by a jailer or coroner an execution issuing upon any judgment therein must be directed to such jailer or coroner. *Boaz v. Nail*, 2 Met. 245. (But see ante, p. 215, Act, February 16, 1864.)

II. *Levy and sale—validity of.*

2. *Levy and release thereof—subsequent levy.*—A sheriff and constable levied executions on the personal property of the defendant. At the instance of the defendant, the officers released the property to him upon his agreeing to sell it and apply the proceeds to the respective executions, according to the liens created by them: *Held*—that after the surrender of the property, the sheriff had a right to levy the executions on the lands of the defendant. *Jones v. Lusk*, 2 Met. 356.

3. *Execution on replevin bond taken for six months void.*—An execution issued upon a replevin bond taken for six instead of three months, is invalid, and a sale and conveyance of land thereunder, passes no title. *Vertrees v. Shean*, 2 Met. 291.

4. *To make valid levy officer must see property.*—The levy of an execution on personal property not seen by the officer at the time of the levy, is invalid and creates no lien upon the property. *Bradley & Cobb v. Phillips*, MSS. opinion, December 10, 1864.

5. *Sale of personality under fi. fa. void unless property seen by purchasers.* And the sale of the same property not seen by the purchasers at the time of the sale is void. *Ib.*

6. *Void execution—sale under.*—A debtor who surrenders in writing a tract of land to be sold under a void execution against him, is not estopped to controvert the title of the purchaser. The sale and sheriff's deed are void, and the surrender can impart no validity to them. *Geoghegan v. Ditto*, 2 Met. 433

### III. Sale of incumbered property and equitable interest under execution.

7. *Property held under title bond not subject to levy.*—The provisions of article five, chapter thirty-six, Revised Statutes, in relation to the sale of incumbered property under execution do not apply to lands of which the defendant has only the possession and a bond for the title, although the execution issued on a judgment for the purchase money. *Moore v. Simpson*, 3 Met. 349.

8. *The same.*—The equitable interest of a party in real estate of which he holds only the possession and a bond for a title, is not subject to levy and sale under execution. *Ib.*

9. *Otherwise if the holder of the bond surrender the land for levy.*—But where such equitable owner by a written indorsement on the execution surrenders his interest in the land for sale in satisfaction thereof, he will be estopped to deny the title of a purchaser thereof, and compelled, by the proper action, to surrender the possession to such purchaser. *Ib.*

10. *Incumbered property.*—The only interest acquired by the purchaser of a right to redeem incumbered property, at a sale thereof under execution, is a lien for the purchase money and ten per cent. interest per annum thereon from the day of sale. *Forrest v. Phillips*, 2 Met. 194.

11. *Incumbered property—effect of sale.*—The defendant surrendered to the sheriff, by writing, a tract of land to be levied on. The land had, in connection with other property, been previously mortgaged to secure another debt, and the mortgage duly recorded. The defendant neither gave notice of the incumbrance or represented it as unincumbered, and it was sold as unincumbered property. Afterward the mortgage was satisfied by the other property: *Held*—nevertheless that the purchaser only acquired a lien for his bid and ten per cent. interest. *Ib.*

### IV. Indemnity to officers before levy or sale.

12. *When officer may demand indemnifying bond.*—The officer has the right to demand an indemnifying bond before proceeding to levy or make sale of personal property, whenever he, in good faith, and upon the exercise of a sound discretion, doubts whether the property is subject to levy and sale. *Board v. Helm*, 2 Met. 500.

*The same.*—This right is not confined to any particular class of cases, and the existence of such doubt is the sole condition upon which the right to demand the bond depends. *Ib.*

14. *When sheriff justified in refusing to sell.*—Where the sheriff's return of an execution showed that no summons had been served upon the defendant in the execution before the rendition of the judgment on which it issued, and that he had levied the execution with the understanding that the plaintiff was to give an indemnifying bond before the sale which he had failed to do: *Held*—that the sheriff was justified in refusing to make the sale. *Ib.*

15. *Bond must not only be taken but returned.*—Before a sheriff can defend himself under an indemnifying bond, he must show a strict compliance

V. DEVASTAVIT.
VI. FOREIGN ADMINISTRATORS.
VII. OTHER MATTERS.

### I. Granting administration.

1. *Not granted on estate of non-resident*—*when*.—A grant of administration on the estate of a non-resident by the courts of this state is void unless the non-resident left *personal* property here to be administered. *Thumb v. Gresham*, 2 Met. 306.

2. *Lapse of time*.—The lapse of twenty-eight years from the death of a person affords a presumption that there are no outstanding debts against his estate, and therefore no administrator is necessary to a division of slaves among his heirs after that time. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

3. *Not granted after twenty years*.—By the Revised Statutes administration can not be granted after twenty years from the death of the decedent. *Ib*.

4. *Husband's executors may administer on deceased wife's estate*—*when*.—Husband surviving his wife is entitled to her choses in action, and the equitable right thereto passes to his executors; but they can not maintain an ordinary action for them without administering on her estate. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

5. *May recover without administering*.—In equity, cases may arise where the husband's executors will be entitled to the benefit of covenants to the wife without having administered on her estate. *Ib*.

### II. Demand on personal representative.

6. *Where debtor dies pending suit*—Where the defendant in an action for money dies before judgment, the plaintiff must verify his claim and make demand upon the administrator before proceeding to judgment. *Matthews v. Jones*, 2 Met. 254.

7. *Rule on plaintiff, to verify*.—Where an action is revived against a personal representative he may have a rule on the plaintiff to verify his demand, and if the latter refuse, the action will be dismissed without prejudice. *Ib*.

### III. Rights and duties of personal representatives. Power to sell.

8. *Power to sell*—*extent of*.—Where the title of land is devised to the executors with power to sell, they may assign a part thereof to the widow for dower, in consideration of her releasing her dower in the residue, which, in

the absence of collusion, will bind the heirs and creditors. *Harrow v. Johnson*, 3 Met. 578.

9. *Power to two, and one resigns.*—If a testator directs lands to be sold by his two executors, and one of them vacates his office, the power (under the statute), devolves on the other. But if both remain in office, both must concur in the sale. *Wills v. Lewis*, 4 Met. 269.

10. *Suit—in whose name brought.*—Where the sheriff is appointed administrator *de bonis non*, he may sue in his own name on notes executed to the former sheriff, as such, which have come to his hands. *Maraman v. Trunnell*, 3 Met. 156.

*Same.*—If the testator devises his lands to his executor with power to sell, giving the devisee only the proceeds when sold, an action to recover the land must be in the name of the executor, *Jennings v. Monks*, 4 Met. 103.

### IV. Marriage of administratrix.

11. *Vacates the office.*—When an administratrix marries, she ceases to be the personal representative of the intestate, and a suit will not then lie against her to establish a debt against the estate, or to test the question of assets to discharge the debts. *Young v. Duhme*, 4 Met. 239.

### V. Devastavit.

12. *What necessary before proceeding for devastavit.*—Before a creditor can maintain an action against the sureties in the administration bond for a *devastavit*, he must obtain a judgment against the personal representative establishing his debt, and must fix upon him assets sufficient to discharge the debt, or some part of it; but a return of no property on an execution issuing thereon is not necessary. *Young v. Duhme*, 4 Met. 240; *Lee v. Waller*, 3 Met. 61.

13. *The same.*—This is more especially the case under our statute, which requires the creditor to verify his debt in a particular way before it is sued on or paid. *Lee v. Waller*, 3 Met. 61.

*Exception.*—To the general rule *supra* there are exceptions, as where the administrator has received sufficient assets to pay all the debts of the intestate, and becomes insolvent, the creditor might attach the property of his surety, who had become non-resident, without having first got judgment against the administrator. *Ib.*

### VI. Foreign administrators.

14. *May be sued in this state, when.*—A foreign administrator who has received assets of the deceased and removed to this state and settled here, may be sued in the courts of this state by the persons entitled to the assets. The right to sue a foreign administrator here has never been extended further than this. *Baker v. Smith*, 3 Met. 264.

1. The adverse party, or his attorney, must have reasonable Notice. notice, in writing, of the time and place of making the application.

2. If made out of court, the order for removal and petition must be lodged with the clerk within five days.

3. The removal shall be to that county in the circuit, or to an adjoining county in another circuit, which shall best suit the convenience of the parties and their witnesses, in the opinion of the judge making the order, to which there is no valid objection. The affidavit of the party shall only be valid as to the county in which the action is pending; and if he makes objections to any other county, he must support his objection to such other counties by the affidavits of two or more credible, disinterested persons, not of kin to nor of counsel in the case, who must state that they are acquainted with public sentiment in the county or counties objected to, and that it is such as is stated by the applicant for the change.

4. The order may be made subject to such equitable terms and conditions as safety to the rights of the parties may seem to require and the judge in his discretion may prescribe.

5. The order shall be void, unless the party obtaining it does, within ten days, pay to the clerk a sum sufficient to cover the expense of travel in making the removal.

SEC. XII. Immediately on the making or receipt of the order, the clerk shall make out a transcript of the record pertaining to the cause, which, with the original papers therein, he shall, as soon as practicable, carry or send by some discreet person to the clerk of the court to which the cause is removed—the former being responsible for the conduct of the person employed.

SEC. XIII. If the papers are transferred ten days before the first day of the next term of the court to which the cause is removed, it shall stand for trial at that term, otherwise not until the term next succeeding.

SEC. XIV. The parties to any suit may, by consent, have an order in or out of court for its removal to any other court.

SEC. XV. The court to which a cause is removed shall have the same power as to its trial and final disposition as that from which it came, and no exception to the original jurisdiction of the latter shall be allowed in favor of the party obtaining the removal.

# TITLE LVI

## FEEBLE-MINDED CHILDREN.

### 1860.
*February 11.*

AN ACT to establish an institution for the education of idiots and feeble-minded children.

Whereas, There are over five hundred idiotic or feeble-minded persons supported by the State of Kentucky, at an annual expense of about thirty thousand dollars, which is yearly increasing in amount; and there are known to be many others in the state sustained by their friends: And whereas, Schools have been successfully established for the education and training of this imbecile and helpless class, both in Europe and America, by which it has been demonstrated that they are capable of comparatively very great improvement, and that their infirmity may, to a considerable degree, be relieved, and so far that, in many cases, they may become intelligent, useful, and self-sustaining; therefore,

**SEC. I.** That there be, and is hereby, established an institution for the education and training of the feeble-minded children of the commonwealth, under the corporate name and title of "The Commissioners of the Kentucky Institution for the Education and Training of Feeble-minded Children."

**SEC. II.** The commissioners of said institution, to be appointed as hereinafter directed, shall be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate, with all the usual powers of a corporation, necessary to carry out the objects of the establishment of said institution, with perpetual succession; with authority to purchase and hold, or to rent or hire, or to receive by gift or bequest, property, real or personal, for the objects of said institution; with capacity of contracting and of being contracted with, of suing and being sued, of pleading and being impleaded, and of using a common seal, and of altering the same at pleasure.

Institution established.
Commissioners incorporated.
