1. *Alien wife*.—Naturalization being a personal privilege, the alien wife does not become a citizen by the naturalization of her husband. *White v. White*, 2 Met. 185.

2. *Alien can not inherit except under statute*.—By the common law, aliens can not inherit. This law is in force here, except as modified by statute. *White v. White*, 2 Met. 185.

3. *Statute of 1800—two years' residence necessary*.—To inherit land under the act of 1800, the alien must have resided in the state two years, and be a resident thereof at the death of the ancestor. *Ib*.

4. *Preparatory oath, alien can not inherit by taking*.—Taking the preparatory oath does not render an alien a citizen of the United States, or enable him to take by decent, nor will his subsequent naturalization vest him with his ancestor's title which had in the meantime passed to the commonwealth. *Ib*.

5. *Alien wife can not inherit or take dower*.—The law in force before the adoption of the Revised Statutes, did not permit the alien wife to inherit from her husband, or to take dower in his lands. *Ib*.

6. *How alien may receive and hold real estate*.—An alien friend, who has resided in this state two years, may receive, hold, and pass any right to land within the commonwealth during the continuance of his residence after that period. *Yeaker v. Yeaker*, 4 Met. 33.

# TITLE LIX.

## FORCIBLE ENTRY AND DETAINER.

---

1. *Right of entry*.—The statute relating to forcible entries and detainers does not affect the common law right of entry when exercised in a peaceable manner, except so far as the statute itself furnishes a remedy to regain the possession. A person having a right to enter, who makes entry in pursuance of that right, can only be removed by a writ of forcible entry and detainer; not by an action in which *the right of possession* is in issue. *Tucker v. Phillips*, 2 Met. 416.

2. *Writ: who may issue.* A writ of forcible detainer can only be issued by a justice of the peace. Such a writ issued by a judge of the county court, together with all proceedings under it, are void. *Johnson v. Erwin*, 3 Met. 251. (But see title COUNTY JUDGE, 2.)

3. *Law of, a substantial re-enactment of old law.*—The provisions of the Civil Code regulating proceedings of forcible entry and detainer, are a substantial re-enactment of the previous act of 1810. *Belcher v. Barret*, 4 Met. 307.

4. *No judgment upon verdict.*—That the magistrate failed to render a judgment upon a verdict of the jury in the country is no ground for dismissing the traverse in the circuit court. Such judgment not necessary to enable party to maintain a traverse. *Ib.*

1. *What constitutes under act of 1856.*—To bring an assignment, sale or transfer within the operation of the act of 1856, two things are essential: (1) the act must be done in contemplation of insolvency: (2) and with intent to prefer one or more creditors to the exclusion in whole or part of the others. *Hampton v. Morris*, 2 Met. 336; *Millett v. Pattinger*, 4 Met. 213.

2. *Providing pro rata payment not within.*—Two partners convey all their property to trustees, which the latter are to apply to the payment of their individual and firm debts, and if not sufficient to pay all, then the money to be divided *pro rata* among all their creditors: *Held*—not within the statute of 1856. *Hampton v. Morris*, 2 Met. 336.

3. *No advantage to be acquired by creditors over each other.*—By an act which amounts to an assignment under the law of 1856, the creditors become invested with equal rights in the property of the debtor, and neither can acquire any advantage over the other by attachments of the property. *Shouse v. Utterback*, 2 Met. 52; *Given v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

4. *Assignment of note to secure sureties.*—A person aware of his insolvency, assigned a note to his sureties as an indemnity to them for a pre-existing liability, reciting that it was "to pay in part a note to M. B., who holds a note on me and they [i. e. the assignees of the note, my] security:" *Held*—to be within the act of 1856. *Corn v. Simms*, 3 Met. 391.

5. *Same.*—That the assignment *supra* was made in pursuance of a previous promise to the sureties to indemnify them in their suretyship, unless it was of a character to invest the sureties with an equitable right to the fund, would make no difference. *Ib*.

6. *Pre-existing liens.*—Where a transaction amounts to an assignment for the benefit of creditors under the act of 1856, the creditors take the property subject to all valid equities and incumbrances existing at the time the transfer takes place. *Ib*.

7. *Sales contemplated by statute, not void.*—The act of 1856 does not declare the sales, conveyances, etc., mentioned therein to be void, but declares that they shall inure to the benefit of all the creditors of the person making such sale. *Given & Co. v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

8. *Authorizing agent to sell debtor's property.*—Where a person owing more than he was able to pay, executed a power of attorney to one of his creditors whom he owed largely, authorizing him to sell his property of every description, collect debts due him, and pay debts owing by him, and transact

9. *Pre-existing laws not repealed*.—The act of 1856 does not repeal the pre-existing acts against fraudulent conveyances. They embrace distinct classes of cases and provide appropriate remedies for each. *Millett v. Patteringer*, 4 Met. 213.

10. *Same*.—The act of 1856 does not prohibit or afford a remedy for, conveyances that are *merely* fraudulent, although it prohibits the preferences allowed by the pre-existing law. *Ib*.

11. *A case within the act of 1856*.—A debtor owed $12,000, and had property worth $6,000. He conveyed part of his property to one of his creditors in payment of $1,000 of his debt. The residue he conveyed to his sureties in consideration of their agreeing to pay $5,020 for which they were bound: *Held*—that it was within the act of 1856. *Story v. Graham*, 4 Met. 319.

12. *Must sue within six months*.—To cause a sale, mortgage or assignment to inure to the benefit of all the creditors, a petition in equity must be filed for that purpose within six months after the transfer is recorded. The benefit of the act can not be obtained by any proceeding under execution or upon motions upon bonds given to suspend sale under execution. *Wintersmith v. Painter*, 2 Met. 457; *Given & Co. v. Gordon*, 3 Met. 538.

13. *Voluntary conveyance—how far void*.—A voluntary conveyance is void as to the debts of the grantor existing before the conveyance; but not on that account alone as to debts contracted by him afterward. *Todd v. Hartley*, 2 Met. 206.

14. *Part of debt created before and part after conveyance*.—Where a part of the demand was created before and a part after the voluntary conveyance, the conveyance, if not intended to be fraudulent, will be set aside only as to so much of the debt as was created before the conveyance. *Ib*.

1. *Sale of road and franchise.*—The legislature have the constitutional power to authorize the sale of "the road, right of way, franchises, and all property real and personal" of a turnpike company, "upon the application of any creditor of the company whose debt may be due and unsatisfied," and to direct the proceeds to be divided *pro rata* among all the creditors. *Lou., etc., T. Co. v. Ballard, 2 Met. 165.*

2. *The same: parties to suit.*—In an action to subject the road and franchises of a turnpike company to sale if the stockholders are necessary parties, and are numerous, one or more may defend for all. *Ib.*

3. *Land over which road passes: who owns.*—The condemnation of land for a turnpike road only vests in the company the right to use the land for the purposes of their road. The company own the road, but not the ground over which it passes. *Kelly v. Donahoe, 2 Met. 482.*

4. *Proprietor's rights in such land.*—The proprietor retains his exclusive right to the land, and to all mines, quarries, springs of water, etc., and the right to use the same for every purpose not inconsistent with the right of way and the rights and franchises of the corporation. *Ib.*

# TITLE LXI.
## FRAUDS—STATUTE OF.

I. CONTRACTS RELATING TO LAND OR INTEREST THEREIN.
II. SALE OF CROPS AND TREES BEFORE SEVERANCE.
III. AGREEMENT TO PAY DEBT OF ANOTHER.
IV. DEBTS CONTRACTED DURING INFANCY. RATIFICATION.
V. TRUSTS AFFECTED BY THE STATUTE.
VI. HOW THE STATUTE RELIED ON.

### I. Contracts relating to land or interests therein.

1. *Verbal grant of right of way.*—As a right of way is an interest in land, a verbal contract that it shall be granted, can not be enforced. *Hall v. McLeod*, 2 Met. 98.
2. *Agreements to buy land.*—Though the statute speaks only of contracts to sell land, a contract by which one agrees to buy land for another is equally within its operation. *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.
3. *Contracts within, valid for some purposes.*—The statute declares that no action shall be brought on a verbal contract for the sale of land, not that the contract is void. Such contracts are valid for some purposes, as for the defense of a possession acquired in good faith under them. *Harrow v. Johnson*, 3 Met. 583.

### II. Sales of the product of realty before severance.

4. *Standing trees.*—A sale of standing trees, in contemplation of their immediate severance from the soil, is a constructive severance, and they pass as chattels, and the contract, though verbal, is not within the statute. *Byassee v. Reese*, 4 Met. 372.
5. *Same.*—The expression "in contemplation of immediate severance from the soil," is used to distinguish a sale of growing crops or standing trees, which pass no interest in the soil beyond a license to enter and take them, from a contract passing the exclusive right to the land for a period for the purpose of enabling a party to make a profit out of the growth upon it. *Ib.*
6. *Sale of the land before severance.*—If land be sold to a *bona fide* purchaser for a valuable consideration paid before notice of a sale of the trees growing thereon, and before they are severed, the trees would pass with the land to the party purchasing it. *Ib.*

# TITLE CXXXI.
## USURY.

---

—For an act limiting the time to sue for usury paid, to one year, see LIMITATION, 2.

---

1. *Party may recover though he failed to set it up when sued.*—Where a party is sued on a debt containing usury, and the defense is known to him but he fails to set it up and judgment goes against him which he pays, he may afterward bring a district action and recover the usury from the party receiving it. *Chinn v. Mitchell*, 2 Met. 92.

2. *Principal not responsible for usury paid by agent.*—If the obligor in a note authorize the obligee to raise money to pay the same, and the latter do so, paying usurious interest for the sum raised, he can not recover the usury so paid from the obligor, although the obligor authorized it to be paid or sanctioned its payment after it had been done. *Totten v. Cooke*, 2 Met. 275.

3. *Recovery after judgment by default.*—A defendant who has suffered judgment by default for a debt embracing usury, and has paid the same, may nevertheless maintain an action for the recovery of the usury although he knew that the usury was embraced in the judgment at the time it was rendered. *Ross v. Ross*, 3 Met. 274.

38. "Children"—*when it means grand-children*.—The word "children" used in a will, will never be enlarged to include grand-children, unless it is indispensably necessary to effectuate the obvious intent of the testator. *Churchill v. Churchill*, 2 Met. 466.

39. *Same*.—Such a construction is admissible only in two cases: *First*, from necessity when the will would otherwise be inoperative: *Secondly*, where the testator has shown by other words that he did not use the word "children," in its ordinary sense, but in a more extended sense. *Ib*.

40. *Defeasible fee*.—A will gave to a devisee a defeasible fee in land and in a slave, subject to divestiture if she should die without issue, upon which event the fee was to vest in her co-devisees or their children, then living. This was an executory devise, and neither the conveyance of the property by the person holding the defeasible fee, nor the possession of the purchaser, however long continued, before the happening of the contingency upon which the executory devise was to take effect, could defeat the right of the executory devisee. *Nunnally v. ## II. Discrediting witness.

15. *Mode of attacking reputation.*—The regular mode of inquiring into the reputation of a witness is to ask those introduced for that purpose, whether they know the general reputation of the person in question among his neighbors, and what that reputation is. The witness must be able to state what is generally said of the person by those among whom he dwells or is conversant. But it is not essential to the admissibility of such statements that they should come from those who are both neighbors and personally acquainted with the party impeached. They are admissible if made either by those who are acquainted with him, or those who dwell in his immediate neighborhood. *Henderson v. Hayne*, 2 Met. 342.

16. *Same: what jury may consider.*—An instruction which confines the jury, in considering the evidence relating to the character of the impeached witness to such statements respecting his general moral character and principles as have been made by those "among whom he dwells, and to whom he is known," is misleading. It is not necessary that it should come from those who are both neighbors and personally acquainted with the party impeached. *Ib.*

17. *When party may contradict his own witness.*—The right of a party to contradict his own witness by showing that "he has made statements different from his present testimony," does not depend upon the ability of the party to prove in addition, that the testimony of the witness is untrue. But he may contradict him: *first*, by other evidence; *secondly*, by showing that he has made statements different from his present testimony. Either or both modes may be adopted. *Champ v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 17.

18. *The same.*—A party introduces a witness to prove certain facts, but the witness deposes that they did not transpire. He can not, by way of discrediting the witness, prove by others that the witness had on other occasions, in their presence, stated the facts in question—in effect to show that the witness had stated out of court facts which he failed to prove in court. *Ib.*

19. *The same.*—Where a witness does not state any fact prejudicial to the party calling him, but only fails to prove facts supposed to be beneficial to the party, it is not a case within the reason or policy of the rule which allows a witness to be contradicted by evidence that he had previously stated the same facts to others. *Ib.*

20. *Testifying falsely.*—An instruction "That it is the province of the jury to decide upon the credibility of each witness introduced in the cause, and if a witness swears *falsely* in relation to one particular fact, in the case, they have a right to disregard every other fact proven by said witness:" *Held*—on the whole, not to be misleading, though the witness must have knowingly testified falsely to a fact material to the case. *Rutherford v. Commonwealth*, 2 Met. 387.

21. *Interrogatories that tend to disgrace witness.*—A witness is not bound to answer interrogatories which, if affirmatively answered, would subject

1. *Indulgence to constable does not excuse neglect of duty.*—Indulgence without consideration, by the plaintiff in the execution, to a constable who has collected the money thereon, does not excuse the constable for a failure to make a proper return of the execution. *Judy v. Howard*, 2 Met. 44.
2. *Penal statutes strictly construed.*—The statutes providing remedies against constables for failing to return executions are highly penal, and parties who seek to enforce them, must comply strictly with their requirements. *Ib.*

to his wards, who go to reside with their relations. He had filed no inventory of their estates within the time prescribed by law, and had set up title in right of his marriage with their mother, to a portion of the estate claimed by them: *Held*—that the guardian should be removed. *Windsor v. McAtee*, 2 Met. 430.

3. *Suits by guardian*.—A guardian can not sue in his own name for personal property of his ward, unlawfully detained by another. The suit must be in the name of the ward by his guardian or next friend. *Anderson v. Watson*, 3 Met. 509.

4. *Suit by non-resident guardian*.—A foreign guardian desiring to sue for the sale of his ward's land in this state, must first procure an order of the county court of the county where the land lies, authorizing him to act as guardian in this state. *Bell v. Clark*, 2 Met. 573.

5. *Hiring out ward*.—If the hiring out of a ward by his guardian be a breach of his trust or a violation of the rights of his ward, a court of equity would, upon a proper case shown, grant adequate relief. *Clinkinbeard v. Clinkinbeard*, 3 Met. 330.

6. *Who may take ward's earnings*.—A guardian hired out his wards. Their mother, a free colored woman, sued the guardian for their wages: *Held*—that she could not recover. *Ib*.

7. *Ward first paid out of deceased guardian's estate*.—The estate of a ward remaining in the hands of his insolvent guardian at the time of his decease, must be paid before a *pro rata* distribution is made among the general creditors of the deceased. *White v. Carrico*, 2 Met. 232.

8. *Creditor of ward*.—In equity the creditor of a ward may be substituted to his right to sue the estate of the deceased guardian, where the claim of the creditor is such a one as the guardian would be legally bound to pay. *Ib*.

9. *Settlement*—*undue influence over ward*.—A receipt as between parties not standing in fiduciary relations, may be explained by parol. Less effect still will be given to a receipt given by a ward to his guardian upon final settlement, where the minority of the ward has recently ceased, and the dominion of the guardian is still presumed to exist to some extent over his mind. *Clay v. Clay*, 3 Met. 548.

10. *Receipt in full by ward to guardian*.—A receipt for a sum of money in full of all claims upon final settlement between guardian and ward within a month after the latter attained age, and where the ward was nephew to the guardian and had been so situated as not to be acquainted with the management of the estate, will not conclusively bar a re-examination of the guardian's account. *Ib*.

11. *Mingling ward's funds with his own by guardian*.—The equitable doctrine applicable to all trustees, and especially to guardians, is that if they use the trust fund or mingle it with their own, they are liable for interest. *Ib*.

12. *Loaning ward's money without security*.—If the guardian loan his ward's money, taking no security therefor, he can not escape personal responsibility for its loss by showing that the person to whom the loan was

22. *Swearing falsely*: *effect*.—It is error to instruct a jury "that if they believe any witness has sworn falsely and knowingly to any material fact, they are *bound* to disregard his testimony altogether." The jury are to judge his testimony, and give it such weight as, under the circumstances, they think it deserves. *Leton v. Young*, 2 Met. 558.

23. *Good character*: *when evidence of admissible*.—Evidence of the good character of a witness is not admissible where he is only impeached by proof of his having made a different statement on another occasion, or by proof of facts which contradict those stated by him in his testimony. *Vance v. Vance*, 2 Met. 581.

### III. Laws suspending and prohibiting suits.

5. *Obligation of contract, what it is.*—In the cases of *Blair v. Williams*, and *Lapsby v. Brashears* (4 Lit. 35, 47), it was settled that "the legal obligation of a contract consists in the remedy given by law to enforce its performance or to make compensation for the failure to perform it." *Johnson v. Higgins*, 3 Met. 566.

6. *Laws changing terms of court.*—Laws prescribing the terms of court and their jurisdiction, do not relate to the *remedy* for enforcing the contract, but to the tribunals by which the remedy is to be enforced. *Ib.*

7. *Constitution construed.*—Section fifteen of article thirteen of the state constitution prescribes certain general duties for the courts of the state and lays down general rules for the manner of conducting their business, as that their proceedings are to be open and not concealed from the public, that they are not to accept compensation from litigants, and are to deny no one a fair trial, or to delay justice except upon sufficient legal grounds for a continuance. This section relates altogether to the judicial and not to the legislative department of the government, and was not designed to regulate either the terms or jurisdiction of the courts. *Johnson v. Higgins*, 3 Met. 566.

8. *Act May 24, 1861, constitutional.*—The first section of the act of May 24, 1861, entitled "an act to suspend the circuit and other courts in this commonwealth and for other purposes," does not impair the obligation of contracts although it prevents the creditor from getting a judgment for his debt as soon as he could have done under the former law. *Ib.*, and *Barkley v. Glover*, 4 Met. 44. [See the act, p. 133.]

9. *Change of remedy.*—Any attempt by the legislature so to change the remedy as to impair the obligation of a contract is prohibited by the constitution. *Berry v. Ransdall*, 4 Met. 292.

10. *Statutes of limitation.*—A statute of limitation that does not allow a reasonable time after its passage for the commencement of suits on existing causes of action is unconstitutional. Thirty days is not a reasonable time. *Ib.*

### IV. Taking private property for public use.

11. *Land taken for a turnpike.*—The constitution secures to the owner of land taken for public use, just compensation for his property before he can be deprived of it. Its value to *him*, considering its relative position to his other land and the other circumstances which may diminish or enhance its value, can alone afford him a just compensation for its loss. *Robb v. M. & M. T. Co.*, 3 Met. 117.

12. *County debt for benefit of a railroad company.*—The act of the legislature under which the bonds and coupons were issued by the city of Mays-

XXVI.] CONSTITUTIONAL LAW. 111

19. *Same.*—The power of the legislature to subject the property of a corporation to its debts is undeniable. *Ib.*

## VII. *Entitling acts.*

20. *Title of an act held sufficient.*—An act, entitled "an act for the redress of injuries arising from the neglect or misconduct of railroad companies and others," held not to be in conflict with that provision of the constitution which declares that no law shall relate to more than one subject, and that shall be expressed in the title, because it provided for injuries inflicted by natural as well as artificial persons. *Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146.*

21. *Another; the same.*—The title of an act was "an act for the benefit of the Louisville and Oldham Turnpike Road Company." The first section authorized the company to borrow money, and execute mortgages; the second authorized the directors to sell the road and right of way; the third authorized the chancellor to sell the road on the application of a creditor, and the fourth substitutes the purchaser to the rights of the company: *Held*—that it related to but one subject which is sufficiently expressed in the title. *Lou. & O. T. R. Co. v. Ballard, 2 Met. 165.*

22. *Another held sufficient.*—An act was entitled "an act to amend the charter of the Covington and Cincinnati Bridge Company." The first section authorized an increase of the capital stock of the company. The second section authorized the company to sell, and the city of Covington to subscribe $100,000 to the capital stock, and for execution of bonds to be used in paying for the stock, and for the levy of a tax on the property in the city to pay the interest on the bonds: *Held*—that the title was sufficient. *Phillips v. Cov. & Cin. Br. Co., 2 Met. 219.*

23. *General rule on the subject.*—No provision of a statute relating directly or indirectly to the subject expressed in the title, having a natural connection therewith, and not foreign to the same, should be deemed within the inhibition of that provision declaring that a law shall relate to but one subject which shall be expressed in the title. This provision should receive a reasonable and not a technical construction. *Johnson v. Higgins, 3 Met. 566. Phillips v. Cov. & Cin. Br. Co., 2 Met. 219.*

## VIII. *Other matters.*

24. *Decisions on constitutional questions to be adhered to.*—Where the meaning of the constitution on a doubtful question has been once carefully considered and judicially decided, the instrument is thereafter to be received in that sense, and every reason is in favor of a steady adherence to such authoritative interpretation. *Maddox v. Graham, etc., 2 Met. 56.*

25. *Two penalties for same offense.*—Conceding it to be against the spirit of the constitution to inflict more than one punishment for the same offense, still a law which authorizes a civil action for the recovery of punative damages by the personal representative of one killed by the willful neglect of

I.** That if the female, with whom a marriage is intended to be had, is a resident of any county in which there are no officers to grant the license, it shall be lawful for the proper officer of any other county to issue such license according to the requirements of the law now in force, except in regard to residence; and the certificate of the marriage shall be returned to, and recorded in the county from which the license is issued: *Provided*, That the fact that there are no officers authorized to grant license in the county of the residence, shall appear by the affidavit of the parent or guardian of the former, if she is an infant, and by her own and the affidavit of her intended husband, if she is an adult, which affidavits shall be filed by the clerk: *And provided further*, That the application must be made in the nearest county where there are officers authorized to grant the license.

**SEC. II.** This act shall be in force from its passage

### I. *Of wife's choses in action, personalty and slaves.*

1. *What law governs right of husband to wife's movables.*—The right of the husband to the movables, including slaves, owned by the wife at the time of the marriage, is determined by the law of the place where they were married, although they may afterward remove with the property to another domicile. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352.

SEC. III. No divorce shall be granted under this act, except in cases where the lunacy or unsoundness of mind was the result of intemperance (or of an hereditary taint of insanity, which was concealed from the other party at the time of marriage), nor until the questions of fact arising under this act have been tried by a jury, as in cases of trials of issues of fact in equity cases: *Provided*, That in the event the jury returns an affirmative verdict, the divorce shall not be granted until and unless the chancellor shall enter upon the record that he has heard all the testimony, and that in his judgment the verdict is sustained by the evidence.

SEC. IV. Where the alleged lunatic, or person of unsound mind, is in an asylum established by the laws of this state, the case may be tried in the circuit or chancery court of the county where the asylum is located.

3. *Husband's right to deceased wife's slaves.*—By the Revised Statutes, and the previous law of 1846, "further to protect the rights of married women," the husband who survives the wife, is only entitled to a life estate in her slaves. *Townes v. Durbin*, 3 Met. 352; *Hocker v. Gentry*, 3 Met. 463.

4. *Where slaves descended before law of 1846.*—Where the marriage took place in 1830, the husband, upon the death of the wife in 1857, was entitled to slaves by survivorship or as administrator, which had descended to her in 1829. *Anderson v. Smith*, 3 Met. 491.

5. *Right to deceased wife's choses in action.*—Husband surviving his wife is entitled to her choses in action, which right, on his decease, will pass to his executor. *Nunnally v. White*, 3 Met. 584.

**SEC. XI.** In all actions brought originally in the Jefferson court of common pleas, or removed into it by appeal, in which the plaintiff claims damages for any trespass, libel, slander, illegal attachment or distress, breach of marriage promise, or on a bond given to obtain a provisional remedy in an action, and does, on such claim, recover no more than five dollars, the said court may, in its discretion, refuse to award the plaintiff the whole or any part of the costs.

**SEC. XII.** Said court shall have full power to make all proper rules to facilitate its business, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this state; and its judgments and final orders shall be subject to appeals, in like manner as those of circuit courts, and not otherwise.

25. *Language held to create separate estate*.—A deed provides that a trustee shall hold the property for the use and benefit and support of the wife and her children, and shall let her and her children have, use, and enjoy every part and parcel of the property, which is to be controled by her for her comfort and support, and for the support and education of her children: *Held*—to be a separate estate. *Toombs v. Stone*, 2 Met. 520.

26. *Trust estate and separate estate*.—The provisions of the Revised Statutes, concerning estates held in trust, have no application to separate estates which are something more than mere trust estates, and which can only belong to a married woman, though they may be created previous to her marriage. *Ib.*

27. *Separate estate not liable for necessaries*.—The provision of the Revised Statutes which renders the wife's real estate and slaves liable for necessaries, agreed to be paid for by writing signed by her and her husband has no application to her separate estate. *Ib.*

43. *Same.*—In the case *supra* the husband delivered the note to the wife indorsed, "I assign the within note to C. W. (his wife), for satisfactory consideration." No trustee was named, nor was the assignment recorded: *Held*—that as against subsequent creditors, she is entitled to the note. As against prior creditors she is entitled to the value of her potential right of dower in the land. *Ib.*

44. *Antenuptial agreements.*—Executory agreements between husband and wife, made before their marriage, and to be performed during marriage, have frequently been enforced in equity, though void at law. *Maraman v. Maraman*, 4 Met. 84.

45. *Conveyance from husband to wife.*—Conveyances by husband to wife, without a trustee, are valid in equity, although at law the general rule is that both executed and executory contracts between them, without the intervention of a trustee, are void. *Ib.*

III.** That hereafter any free negro or mulatto, not a resident of Kentucky, who shall come to this state for any purpose or upon any pretense whatever, unless in obedience to the process of a court, shall likewise be deemed guilty of a felony, and, upon conviction thereof, shall be confined in the penitentiary for a period of not less than one, nor more than five years.

> Free negro or mulatto coming into the state guilty of felony.

**SEC. III.** That hereafter any free negro or mulatto, not a resident of Kentucky, who shall come to this state for any purpose or upon any pretense whatever, unless in obedience to the process of a court, shall likewise be deemed guilty of a felony, and, upon conviction thereof, shall be confined in the penitentiary for a period of not less than one, nor more than five years.

**SEC. IV.** That when any free negro or mulatto who may be convicted under either of the two preceding sections of this act, shall have served out one fourth of the time for which he or she was sentenced, any person, as principal, shall have the right to execute a bond to the commonwealth, with at least one good resident security, to be approved by the governor, conditioned that such convict will, within ten days, leave this state and never return; and thereupon it shall be the duty of the governor to deposit such bond in the office of the secretary of state, and cause the keeper of the penitentiary to be notified that the same has been executed; immediately upon the receipt of such notice, it shall be the duty of the keeper of the penitentiary to discharge such convict, as though the time for which he or she was sentenced had expired.

V.** That if any convict, who has been discharged as provided in the preceding section, shall leave the state and afterward return, or shall fail to leave within the time prescribed, unless unavoidably prevented, he or she shall be punished by confinement in the penitentiary during life; and, in any such case, suit may be brought upon said bond in the circuit court of Franklin county, by the attorney general in the name of the commonwealth, for the recovery of such damages as may be due, which damages shall in no event be less than the value of the free negro or mulatto at the time the bond was executed; the attorney general shall receive twenty per cent. upon all amounts recovered under the provisions of this section, and the remainder shall go to the common school fund.

11. *Order for restitution of property*—*when made*.—It is not necessary that the order for the restoration of property obtained by either party from the other during the marriage, shall be made at the time the judgment of divorce is rendered, but it may be made and enforced at a subsequent time. *Williams v. Gooch*, 3 Met. 486.

12. *To be made though not prayed for in pleadings*.—The order of restoration in a judgment of divorce contemplated by Code, section four hundred and sixty-two, was a formal one which the law directs to be made in every final judgment of divorce from the bond of matrimony, whether the pleadings demand such relief or not. *Ib.*

AN ACT to amend section nine, chapter forty-seven, Revised Statutes, title "Husband and Wife."*

SEC. I. That no person shall solemnize marriage until, in addition to the present requirements of law, he shall file in the office of the county court of the county of his residence, a written affidavit subscribed by him, and sworn to before some person legally authorized to administer an oath, of the following purport and effect, viz: "I do solemnly swear (or affirm, as the case may be), that I will support the constitution of the United States, and the constitution of this state, and be faithful and true to the Commonwealth of Kentucky, and the laws and government thereof, so long as I continue a citizen thereof; and I do further solemnly swear, or affirm, that I will not aid, assist, abet, or comfort, directly or indirectly, the so-called Confederate States, or those now in rebellion against the United States, or the State of Kentucky, so long as I continue a citizen of this state, so help me God."

SEC. II. That any person who shall, after this act takes effect, solemnize marriage, without first complying with the provisions of the first section of this act, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and shall be subject to a fine, for every such offense, upon the presentment of a grand jury, of not less than fifty nor more than five hundred dollars.

3. He shall cause the lists of births, of deaths, and of marriages to be securely stitched in pasteboard in separate volumes, convenient for reference, whenever the number of each shall have accumulated so as to form a properly sized volume, taking care that the different years be placed in regular order as to time. He shall attach to each volume an alphabetical index of each name of a white person found therein. He shall carefully preserve at least one copy of each annual report in his office for convenient reference. He shall see that all blanks relating to the registration of births, etc., sent to his office by the registrar, are properly distributed. And he shall deliver to the assessor, along with the blanks prepared for his use by the registrar, the certificates of physicians, etc., the better to enable him to make a correct list of births and deaths. Along with his copy of the assessor's book, he shall send to the auditor a statement of the names of each physician, surgeon, and midwife who shall have complied with the duties required of them by this act.

**FEES.**

**SEC. V.** The registrar shall receive for his services, for superintending the whole subject of registration, and for making the annual report, the sum of fifteen hundred dollars, to be paid annually upon the completion of the report. The clerks of county courts shall receive the same pay for copying the lists of births, marriages, and deaths, and transmitting them to the auditor, as they receive for copying and forwarding the lists of taxable property. The assessor shall receive ten cents for each list faithfully recorded in the regular discharge of his duty, and ten cents for each birth or death which shall have occurred in former years, or in families then residing beyond the limits of the state. This last price shall be paid by the person at whose instance the entry is made.

AN ACT to amend an act to protect graves and graveyards. 1

SEC. I. The second section of an act, approved March 9, 1854, entitled "an act to protect graves and graveyards," is hereby so amended as to allow the surviving husband of a deceased wife to remove her remains, or that of any child or children of the marriage, from a private burying-ground to a public cemetery; and he may take the same steps to effect the object desired by him as is provided in said section for entering upon the ground of any private graveyard for making repairs.

SEC. II. This act to be in force from its passage.

- Guardians who engage in rebellion to be removed. See REBELLION AND INSURRECTION, 4.

1. *Guardians for free negro children.*—The power of the county court to appoint and remove guardians and curators for minors who are free persons of color, can not be questioned under the statutes concerning guardians and wards. *Clinkinbeard v. Clinkinbeard*, 3 Met. 330.

2. *Removal of guardian.*—A step-father was appointed guardian of his step-daughters. Their mother dies, and he intermarries with a lady stranger

[TITLE

to his wards, who go to reside with their relations. He had filed no inventory of their estates within the time prescribed by law, and had set up title in right of his marriage with their mother, to a portion of the estate claimed by them: *Held*—that the guardian should be removed. *Windsor v. McAtee*, 2 Met. 430.

3. *Suits by guardian*.—A guardian can not sue in his own name for personal property of his ward, unlawfully detained by another. The suit must be in the name of the ward by his guardian or next friend. *Anderson v. Watson*, 3 Met. 509.

5. *Hiring out ward*.—If the hiring out of a ward by his guardian be a breach of his trust or a violation of the rights of his ward, a court of equity would, upon a proper case shown, grant adequate relief. *Clinkinbeard v. Clinkinbeard*, 3 Met. 330.

6. *Who may take ward's earnings*.—A guardian hired out his wards. Their mother, a free colored woman, sued the guardian for their wages: *Held*—that she could not recover. *Ib*.

7. *Ward first paid out of deceased guardian's estate*.—The estate of a ward remaining in the hands of his insolvent guardian at the time of his decease, must be paid before a *pro rata* distribution is made among the general creditors of the deceased. *White v. Carrico*, 2 Met. 232.

AN ACT to legalize certain marriages in this commonwealth. 1

Whereas, It is represented to this general assembly, that doubts are entertained as to the legality of certain marriages in this commonwealth, under license obtained recently from persons claiming to be county clerks, but were not so in fact, and had no legal right to issue such license; and whereas, it is represented that the persons thus married, entered into that relation in good faith, and with the intention of living in all the ties and obligations to each other of husband and wife; therefore, for remedy,

SEC. I. That all marriages in this commonwealth, under such spurious license, from the first day of December, 1861, to the passage of this act, be, and the same are hereby, made legal and valid: *Provided*, This act shall not be construed so as to exempt ministers or other persons who have solemnized the right of marriage in such cases from returning the certificate of marriage as now required by law.
